</svg>`,
  brainLobes:`<svg viewBox="0 0 820 470" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<defs>
 <linearGradient id="gF" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#9C92E0"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#7B6FD4"/></linearGradient>
 <linearGradient id="gP" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#9FC6A9"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#6FA67B"/></linearGradient>
 <linearGradient id="gO" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#9DBEDD"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#6E97C6"/></linearGradient>
 <linearGradient id="gT" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#E6C079"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#D7A24C"/></linearGradient>
 <clipPath id="brain"><path d="M70 250 C70 160 140 96 250 92 C360 88 450 92 540 112 C636 133 726 156 744 224 C756 268 742 306 700 320 C690 350 654 356 612 350 C566 372 470 384 398 378 C346 374 300 366 286 336 C250 352 176 350 138 322 C96 300 70 292 70 250 Z"/></clipPath>
</defs>
<!-- base -->
<path d="M70 250 C70 160 140 96 250 92 C360 88 450 92 540 112 C636 133 726 156 744 224 C756 268 742 306 700 320 C690 350 654 356 612 350 C566 372 470 384 398 378 C346 374 300 366 286 336 C250 352 176 350 138 322 C96 300 70 292 70 250 Z" fill="#EFE9F6"/>
<g clip-path="url(#brain)">
 <!-- lobe regions (organic-ish overlays) -->
 <path d="M60 250 C70 150 150 95 250 92 L300 92 C300 200 290 300 286 336 C250 352 176 350 138 322 C96 300 60 292 60 250 Z" fill="url(#gF)" opacity="0.85"/>
 <path d="M300 92 C390 90 470 96 520 110 L520 250 C460 250 360 250 300 250 Z" fill="url(#gP)" opacity="0.8"/>
 <path d="M520 110 C620 128 726 156 744 224 C756 268 742 308 700 322 C660 318 600 300 566 288 C540 282 522 280 520 270 L520 110 Z" fill="url(#gO)" opacity="0.82"/>
 <path d="M284 322 C360 302 470 298 566 288 C588 308 600 330 612 352 C566 374 470 384 398 378 C346 374 300 366 284 334 Z" fill="url(#gT)" opacity="0.86"/>
 <!-- gyri texture -->
 <g stroke="#ffffff" stroke-width="2.2" fill="none" opacity="0.30" stroke-linecap="round">
  <path d="M110 160 q40 -22 78 -4 M100 195 q44 -22 88 -2 M104 230 q44 -20 90 0 M120 270 q44 -18 88 2 M150 300 q40 -14 80 4"/>
  <path d="M330 130 q42 -18 84 0 M335 165 q42 -16 84 2 M340 205 q42 -14 82 4"/>
  <path d="M560 150 q44 -16 86 2 M575 188 q42 -14 82 4 M585 225 q40 -12 78 6 M600 262 q38 -10 74 8"/>
  <path d="M330 300 q46 16 92 2 M360 330 q46 14 92 0 M410 352 q44 10 88 -2"/>
 </g>
 <!-- central sulcus -->
 <path d="M300 96 C312 170 300 240 286 332" fill="none" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="3.4" opacity="0.62"/>
 <!-- lateral (Sylvian) fissure -->
 <path d="M286 320 C360 300 470 296 560 286" fill="none" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="3.4" opacity="0.62"/>
 <!-- parieto-occipital -->
 <path d="M520 112 C516 180 516 230 520 268" fill="none" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.6" opacity="0.5" stroke-dasharray="2 6"/>
</g>
<path d="M70 250 C70 160 140 96 250 92 C360 88 450 92 540 112 C636 133 726 156 744 224 C756 268 742 306 700 320 C690 350 654 356 612 350 C566 372 470 384 398 378 C346 374 300 366 286 336 C250 352 176 350 138 322 C96 300 70 292 70 250 Z" fill="none" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="3.4"/>
<!-- cerebellum -->
<path d="M612 350 C690 356 742 322 724 280 C690 300 700 330 660 332 C636 334 622 342 612 350 Z" fill="#DD8468" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.6"/>
<g stroke="#fff" stroke-width="1.1" opacity="0.55" fill="none"><path d="M636 332 q40 -6 78 -22 M644 342 q40 -6 74 -20 M654 350 q34 -6 64 -18"/></g>
<!-- brainstem -->
<path d="M398 378 C404 420 420 446 408 466 C392 446 384 414 380 380 Z" fill="#5E6E88" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.6"/>
<!-- labels -->
<g font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-weight="800" font-size="16">
<text x="150" y="200" fill="#fff">Frontal</text>
<text x="350" y="180" fill="#fff">Parietal</text>
<text x="588" y="200" fill="#fff">Occipital</text>
<text x="360" y="340" fill="#7A5A2E">Temporal</text>
</g>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10.5" fill="#fff" opacity="0.92">
<text x="150" y="218">planning · movement</text>
<text x="350" y="198">touch · space</text>
<text x="588" y="218">vision</text>
</g>
<text x="360" y="357" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10.5" fill="#7A5A2E">hearing · memory</text>
<g font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-weight="700" font-size="12.5">
<text x="636" y="392" fill="#DD8468">Cerebellum</text>
<text x="430" y="452" fill="#5E6E88">Brainstem</text>
</g>
<line x1="660" y1="388" x2="686" y2="338" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="1.2"/>
<line x1="426" y1="448" x2="404" y2="448" stroke="#5E6E88" stroke-width="1.2"/>
<text x="300" y="78" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#324A6D" opacity="0.7" transform="rotate(74 300 78)">central sulcus</text>
<text x="70" y="455" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#9BA6BA">◀ FRONT</text>
<text x="775" y="408" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#9BA6BA" text-anchor="end">BACK ▶</text>
</svg>`,
  subcortical:`<svg viewBox="0 0 820 510" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<defs><radialGradient id="cortex" cx="0.4" cy="0.32" r="0.85"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#F6EFF6"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#E9E3F6"/></radialGradient></defs>
<path d="M60 250 C58 150 150 70 300 60 C470 48 620 70 720 150 C772 192 770 250 740 300 C716 342 662 358 600 356 C520 360 360 360 220 356 C140 352 96 340 80 300 C64 282 60 268 60 250 Z" fill="url(#cortex)" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="3.2"/>
<g stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="1.4" fill="none" opacity="0.4">
<path d="M120 140 q30 -22 64 -8 M100 182 q34 -22 70 -6 M300 80 q40 -20 80 -2 M470 78 q40 -18 80 0 M610 96 q40 -16 78 2 M690 152 q32 -12 60 6"/></g>
<!-- corpus callosum -->
<path d="M205 256 C215 180 330 150 450 150 C545 150 612 168 648 210 C612 188 545 178 452 180 C345 180 250 200 232 262 Z" fill="#5E6E88" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.6"/>
<!-- fornix -->
<path d="M250 282 C300 242 420 234 466 256" fill="none" stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<!-- basal ganglia (lateral, behind) -->
<ellipse cx="300" cy="244" rx="38" ry="30" fill="#6E97C6" opacity="0.5" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
<!-- thalamus -->
<ellipse cx="402" cy="250" rx="56" ry="42" fill="#7B6FD4" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.8"/>
<!-- hypothalamus -->
<ellipse cx="392" cy="306" rx="29" ry="18" fill="#D7A24C" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.8"/>
<!-- pituitary -->
<line x1="388" y1="322" x2="388" y2="332" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.4"/>
<circle cx="388" cy="340" r="10" fill="#E6C079" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.5"/>
<!-- amygdala -->
<circle cx="300" cy="318" r="18" fill="#DD8468" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.8"/>
<!-- hippocampus -->
<path d="M338 322 C382 320 390 356 354 372 C382 360 370 338 338 344 Z" fill="#6FA67B" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.8"/>
<!-- brainstem stalk -->
<path d="M448 338 C472 350 486 382 488 414 C490 446 500 470 494 494 C476 472 460 442 452 410 C446 382 444 358 442 340 Z" fill="#CCC6DD" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.8"/>
<g stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.4"><path d="M452 372 q24 4 40 2 M456 412 q24 4 42 2"/></g>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#5E6E88"><text x="506" y="368">midbrain</text><text x="506" y="406">pons</text><text x="506" y="452">medulla</text></g>
<!-- cerebellum -->
<path d="M598 356 C690 358 744 312 722 266 C702 298 700 330 652 336 C626 340 610 348 598 356 Z" fill="#DD8468" opacity="0.9" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.2"/>
<g stroke="#fff" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.55" fill="none"><path d="M626 336 q44 -8 88 -30 M634 346 q44 -8 82 -26 M646 353 q36 -8 68 -22"/></g>
<!-- labels -->
<g font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-weight="800" font-size="14">
<text x="34" y="150" fill="#5E6E88">Corpus callosum</text>
<text x="34" y="250" fill="#7B6FD4">Thalamus</text>
<text x="34" y="318" fill="#D7A24C">Hypothalamus</text>
<text x="34" y="384" fill="#B8862F">Pituitary</text>
<text x="120" y="466" fill="#6E97C6">Basal ganglia</text>
<text x="250" y="492" fill="#DD8468">Amygdala</text>
<text x="372" y="492" fill="#6FA67B">Hippocampus</text>
<text x="700" y="412" fill="#DD8468">Cerebellum</text>
<text x="556" y="492" fill="#5E6E88">Brainstem</text></g>
<g stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none" opacity="0.7">
<path d="M148 146 L300 176"/><path d="M120 246 L346 250"/><path d="M150 314 L364 306"/>
<path d="M104 380 L378 340"/><path d="M180 460 L292 256"/>
<path d="M286 484 L300 336"/><path d="M410 484 L360 360"/><path d="M698 408 L668 348"/><path d="M554 488 L480 440"/></g>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">
<text x="34" y="166">hemisphere bridge</text><text x="34" y="266">sensory relay</text>
<text x="34" y="334">homeostasis · hormones</text><text x="700" y="428">balance · timing</text></g>
</svg>`,
  neuron:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<defs><marker id="ah" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#7B6FD4"/></marker></defs>
<line x1="120" y1="30" x2="600" y2="30" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#ah)" opacity="0.6"/>
<text x="300" y="22" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#7B6FD4">signal travels this way →</text>
<!-- dendrites -->
<g stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round">
<path d="M150 160 C110 120 80 110 55 95 M120 130 C95 110 75 100 60 80"/>
<path d="M150 160 C110 160 80 160 50 155 M125 150 C100 150 80 152 58 150"/>
<path d="M150 175 C110 200 85 215 60 230 M125 188 C100 205 82 215 62 225"/>
</g>
<!-- soma -->
<ellipse cx="180" cy="170" rx="46" ry="40" fill="#ECE9FB" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2.5"/>
<circle cx="180" cy="170" r="17" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<!-- axon -->
<line x1="226" y1="170" x2="560" y2="170" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<!-- myelin beads -->
<g fill="#D7A24C" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.5">
<ellipse cx="270" cy="170" rx="26" ry="15"/><ellipse cx="340" cy="170" rx="26" ry="15"/>
<ellipse cx="410" cy="170" rx="26" ry="15"/><ellipse cx="480" cy="170" rx="26" ry="15"/></g>
<!-- terminals -->
<g stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="4" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round">
<path d="M560 170 C590 150 610 140 630 135 M560 170 C590 170 615 170 635 170 M560 170 C590 190 610 200 630 210"/></g>
<circle cx="634" cy="134" r="7" fill="#DD8468"/><circle cx="638" cy="170" r="7" fill="#DD8468"/><circle cx="634" cy="210" r="7" fill="#DD8468"/>
<!-- next dendrite -->
<path d="M700 120 C690 160 690 185 700 225" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="4" fill="none"/>
<g fill="#DD8468" opacity="0.8"><circle cx="660" cy="150" r="3"/><circle cx="668" cy="170" r="3"/><circle cx="662" cy="190" r="3"/></g>
<!-- labels -->
<g font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-weight="800" font-size="13" fill="#324A6D">
<text x="30" y="265" fill="#7B6FD4">Dendrites</text>
<text x="150" y="240" fill="#7B6FD4">Soma</text>
<text x="300" y="125" fill="#D7A24C">Myelin sheath</text>
<text x="385" y="220" fill="#324A6D">Axon</text>
<text x="558" y="255" fill="#DD8468">Axon terminals</text>
<text x="688" y="255" fill="#6FA67B">Synapse</text>
</g>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">
<text x="30" y="280">receive signals</text>
<text x="150" y="255">cell body + nucleus</text>
<text x="300" y="139">insulates &amp; speeds</text>
<text x="558" y="270">release messengers</text>
</g>
<text x="305" y="200" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#324A6D">node of Ranvier</text>
<line x1="305" y1="185" x2="305" y2="172" stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="1"/>
</svg>`,
  actionPotential:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="90" y1="60" x2="90" y2="310" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<line x1="90" y1="310" x2="710" y2="310" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="60" y="50" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="11" fill="#5E6E88">mV</text>
<text x="690" y="335" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="11" fill="#5E6E88">time →</text>
<!-- gridlines -->
<g stroke="#EBE3EC" stroke-width="1">
<line x1="90" y1="77" x2="710" y2="77"/><line x1="90" y1="180" x2="710" y2="180"/>
</g>
<line x1="90" y1="240" x2="710" y2="240" stroke="#D7A24C" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 5"/>
<line x1="90" y1="266" x2="710" y2="266" stroke="#5E6E88" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="3 6"/>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">
<text x="50" y="81">+40</text><text x="62" y="184">0</text>
<text x="44" y="244" fill="#D7A24C">−55</text><text x="44" y="270">−70</text>
</g>
<text x="100" y="258" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#D7A24C">threshold</text>
<text x="610" y="262" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">resting −70mV</text>
<!-- curve -->
<path d="M100 266 L200 266 Q230 264 250 240 C265 222 275 90 300 80 C320 73 335 120 350 200 C360 250 370 285 390 285 C410 285 430 274 470 268 C520 262 660 266 700 266"
 fill="none" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3.5" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<!-- phase labels -->
<g font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="11.5" font-weight="700">
<text x="120" y="300" fill="#5E6E88">1 · Resting</text>
<text x="255" y="70" fill="#DD8468">2 · Depolarisation</text>
<text x="360" y="150" fill="#6E97C6">3 · Repolarisation</text>
<text x="400" y="305" fill="#6FA67B">4 · Hyperpolarisation</text>
</g>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#9BA6BA">
<text x="255" y="84">Na⁺ rushes in</text><text x="360" y="164">K⁺ flows out</text>
</g>
</svg>`,
  synapse:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<!-- presynaptic -->
<path d="M120 30 H640 V120 C640 150 600 165 560 165 H260 C200 165 120 155 120 110 Z" fill="#ECE9FB" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2.5"/>
<text x="135" y="55" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4">Presynaptic terminal (axon)</text>
<!-- vesicles -->
<g fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2">
<circle cx="240" cy="95" r="20"/><circle cx="330" cy="105" r="20"/><circle cx="430" cy="92" r="20"/></g>
<g fill="#DD8468"><circle cx="234" cy="90" r="3"/><circle cx="246" cy="98" r="3"/><circle cx="240" cy="100" r="3"/>
<circle cx="324" cy="100" r="3"/><circle cx="336" cy="108" r="3"/><circle cx="424" cy="88" r="3"/><circle cx="436" cy="96" r="3"/></g>
<text x="240" y="68" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#7B6FD4" text-anchor="middle">vesicles</text>
<!-- cleft + NT -->
<g fill="#DD8468"><circle cx="300" cy="195" r="4"/><circle cx="340" cy="210" r="4"/><circle cx="380" cy="200" r="4"/><circle cx="420" cy="215" r="4"/><circle cx="470" cy="198" r="4"/><circle cx="510" cy="208" r="4"/></g>
<text x="600" y="200" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#DD8468" text-anchor="middle">neurotransmitters</text>
<text x="600" y="185" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="middle">synaptic cleft</text>
<!-- postsynaptic -->
<path d="M120 330 H640 V250 C640 230 600 235 480 240 C360 245 200 235 120 250 Z" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2.5"/>
<text x="135" y="312" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#6FA67B">Postsynaptic membrane (dendrite)</text>
<!-- receptors -->
<g fill="#fff" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2">
<path d="M320 248 h28 v-14 h-9 v9 h-10 v-9 h-9 Z"/><path d="M400 246 h28 v-14 h-9 v9 h-10 v-9 h-9 Z"/>
<path d="M480 248 h28 v-14 h-9 v9 h-10 v-9 h-9 Z"/></g>
<text x="414" y="278" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#6FA67B" text-anchor="middle">receptors</text>
<!-- reuptake arrow -->
<path d="M200 200 C170 200 165 150 200 130" fill="none" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/>
<text x="150" y="175" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">reuptake</text>
</svg>`,
  brakeAccel:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="380" y1="60" x2="380" y2="200" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<polygon points="340,200 420,200 380,150" fill="#324A6D"/>
<rect x="120" y="120" width="200" height="60" rx="14" fill="#FBE7DD" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="2"/>
<rect x="440" y="120" width="200" height="60" rx="14" fill="#E4EDF7" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2"/>
<line x1="180" y1="120" x2="580" y2="120" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<text x="220" y="148" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="16" font-weight="800" fill="#DD8468" text-anchor="middle">Glutamate</text>
<text x="220" y="168" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="11" fill="#DD8468" text-anchor="middle">accelerator · excite</text>
<text x="540" y="148" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="16" font-weight="800" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">GABA</text>
<text x="540" y="168" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="11" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">brake · inhibit</text>
<text x="380" y="40" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D" text-anchor="middle">A healthy brain keeps these in balance</text>
</svg>`,
  devTimeline:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="90" y1="150" x2="690" y2="150" stroke="#EBE3EC" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<line x1="90" y1="150" x2="690" y2="150" stroke="url(#dg)" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round"/>
<defs><linearGradient id="dg"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#7B6FD4"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#DD8468"/></linearGradient></defs>
<circle cx="120" cy="150" r="30" fill="#7B6FD4" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="3"/><text x="120" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24">🧬</text><text x="120" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Prenatal</text><text x="120" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">neural tube</text>
<circle cx="263" cy="150" r="30" fill="#6E97C6" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="3"/><text x="263" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24">👶</text><text x="263" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Infancy</text><text x="263" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">synapse boom</text>
<circle cx="405" cy="150" r="30" fill="#6FA67B" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="3"/><text x="405" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24">🧒</text><text x="405" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Childhood</text><text x="405" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">myelination</text>
<circle cx="548" cy="150" r="30" fill="#D7A24C" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="3"/><text x="548" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24">🧑</text><text x="548" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Adolescence</text><text x="548" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">pruning + PFC</text>
<circle cx="670" cy="150" r="30" fill="#DD8468" stroke="#fff" stroke-width="3"/><text x="670" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="24">🧓</text><text x="670" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Adulthood</text><text x="670" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#5E6E88">refinement</text>
</svg>`,
  synapticDensity:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="80" y1="40" x2="80" y2="270" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<line x1="80" y1="270" x2="710" y2="270" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="20" y="150" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88" transform="rotate(-90 30 150)">synaptic density</text>
<path d="M80 250 C150 250 180 80 260 70 C340 62 360 80 440 110 C540 150 620 175 700 178" fill="none" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3.5"/>
<path d="M80 250 C150 250 180 80 260 70 C340 62 360 80 440 110 C540 150 620 175 700 178 L700 270 L80 270 Z" fill="#ECE9FB" opacity="0.5"/>
<line x1="270" y1="40" x2="270" y2="270" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="1.3" stroke-dasharray="4 5"/>
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">
<text x="100" y="290">Birth</text><text x="245" y="290">~2 yrs</text><text x="430" y="290">childhood</text><text x="560" y="290">adolescence</text><text x="660" y="290">adult</text>
</g>
<text x="160" y="60" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4">overproduction</text>
<text x="420" y="80" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#DD8468">pruning</text>
<text x="280" y="56" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#DD8468">peak density</text>
</svg>`,
  ltp:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 280" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<text x="190" y="36" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="middle">Before, weak connection</text>
<text x="570" y="36" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#6FA67B" text-anchor="middle">After repetition, strengthened</text>
<!-- left pair -->
<circle cx="110" cy="150" r="34" fill="#ECE9FB" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="110" cy="150" r="12" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<circle cx="280" cy="150" r="34" fill="#ECE9FB" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="280" cy="150" r="12" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<line x1="144" y1="150" x2="246" y2="150" stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="2"/>
<g fill="#DD8468" opacity="0.6"><circle cx="195" cy="150" r="3"/></g>
<!-- arrow -->
<path d="M340 150 H420" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#ah2)"/>
<defs><marker id="ah2" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#324A6D"/></marker></defs>
<text x="380" y="140" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#324A6D" text-anchor="middle">fire</text>
<text x="380" y="172" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#324A6D" text-anchor="middle">together</text>
<!-- right pair -->
<circle cx="490" cy="150" r="34" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="490" cy="150" r="12" fill="#6FA67B"/>
<circle cx="660" cy="150" r="34" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="660" cy="150" r="12" fill="#6FA67B"/>
<line x1="524" y1="150" x2="626" y2="150" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="6"/>
<g fill="#DD8468"><circle cx="560" cy="143" r="4"/><circle cx="575" cy="157" r="4"/><circle cx="590" cy="145" r="4"/><circle cx="605" cy="156" r="4"/></g>
<text x="380" y="250" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D" text-anchor="middle">"Neurons that fire together, wire together", long-term potentiation</text>
</svg>`,
  plasticityTypes:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 280" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<rect x="20" y="20" width="350" height="240" rx="16" fill="#ECE9FB" opacity="0.5"/>
<rect x="390" y="20" width="350" height="240" rx="16" fill="#E4EDF7" opacity="0.5"/>
<text x="195" y="50" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4" text-anchor="middle">Structural</text>
<text x="195" y="70" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="middle">physical growth of connections</text>
<text x="565" y="50" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="800" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">Functional</text>
<text x="565" y="70" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="middle">a new region takes over a job</text>
<!-- structural: a neuron sprouting -->
<circle cx="160" cy="170" r="30" fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="160" cy="170" r="11" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<g stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round">
<path d="M190 170 H250 M250 170 l20 -18 M250 170 l20 18 M250 170 l24 0"/>
<path d="M160 140 l-20 -22 M160 140 l8 -26 M135 150 l-26 -10" opacity="0.9"/></g>
<g stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="3" fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-dasharray="2 5">
<path d="M270 152 l26 -16 M270 188 l26 16 M274 170 l26 0"/></g>
<text x="300" y="232" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#6FA67B" text-anchor="middle">new branches grow</text>
<!-- functional: damaged region rerouting -->
<rect x="450" y="120" width="70" height="60" rx="10" fill="#ddd" stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/>
<text x="485" y="155" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="middle">damaged</text>
<rect x="600" y="120" width="70" height="60" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2.5"/>
<text x="635" y="155" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">healthy</text>
<path d="M520 150 C555 110 575 110 600 150" fill="none" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#ah3)"/>
<defs><marker id="ah3" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#6E97C6"/></marker></defs>
<text x="565" y="232" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="middle">function reassigned</text>
</svg>`,
  reflexArc:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<text x="40" y="40" font-size="30">🔥</text>
<text x="30" y="80" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#DD8468">painful stimulus</text>
<path d="M70 70 C70 110 90 150 150 150" fill="none" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#ar)"/>
<defs><marker id="ar" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#324A6D"/></marker></defs>
<!-- spinal cord cross-section -->
<ellipse cx="380" cy="150" rx="80" ry="95" fill="#EFE9F6" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.5"/>
<path d="M340 95 C360 120 360 180 340 205 C410 215 420 120 380 95 C360 90 348 90 340 95 Z" fill="#5E6E88" opacity="0.5"/>
<text x="380" y="265" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">spinal cord</text>
<!-- sensory neuron -->
<path d="M150 150 L300 130" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#ar)"/>
<circle cx="150" cy="150" r="7" fill="#6E97C6"/>
<text x="175" y="120" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#6E97C6">sensory (afferent)</text>
<!-- interneuron -->
<circle cx="380" cy="150" r="9" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<text x="380" y="175" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#7B6FD4">interneuron</text>
<!-- motor neuron -->
<path d="M300 175 L150 200 C100 210 90 240 90 250" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#ar)" fill="none"/>
<text x="160" y="225" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#6FA67B">motor (efferent)</text>
<!-- muscle -->
<rect x="40" y="250" width="90" height="34" rx="10" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="85" y="272" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#6FA67B">muscle pulls away</text>
<text x="520" y="120" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">The reflex is handled</text>
<text x="520" y="142" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">in the cord, you move</text>
<text x="520" y="164" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">before the brain feels pain.</text>
</svg>`,
  visualPathway:`<svg viewBox="0 0 800 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<defs><marker id="v" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#7B6FD4"/></marker></defs>
<!-- eyes -->
<ellipse cx="70" cy="120" rx="26" ry="20" fill="#fff" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="58" cy="120" r="8" fill="#324A6D"/>
<ellipse cx="70" cy="230" rx="26" ry="20" fill="#fff" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="58" cy="230" r="8" fill="#324A6D"/>
<text x="44" y="160" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">eyes</text>
<!-- optic nerves to chiasm -->
<path d="M96 120 L210 165" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3"/><path d="M96 230 L210 195" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="3"/>
<!-- chiasm -->
<circle cx="220" cy="180" r="12" fill="#fff" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="220" y="215" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#324A6D">optic chiasm</text>
<!-- cross to LGN -->
<path d="M232 180 L330 180" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#v)"/>
<ellipse cx="360" cy="180" rx="28" ry="20" fill="#7B6FD4" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="360" y="185" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#fff">LGN</text>
<text x="360" y="220" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#7B6FD4">thalamus relay</text>
<!-- optic radiation to V1 -->
<path d="M388 180 L470 180" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#v)"/>
<path d="M470 140 C540 120 560 240 470 220 C520 200 520 160 470 140 Z" fill="#6E97C6" opacity="0.85" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="505" y="184" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#fff">V1</text>
<text x="505" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#6E97C6">primary visual cortex</text>
<!-- two streams -->
<path d="M540 160 C620 110 650 90 700 90" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="3" fill="none" marker-end="url(#v)"/>
<path d="M540 200 C620 250 650 270 700 270" stroke="#D7A24C" stroke-width="3" fill="none" marker-end="url(#v)"/>
<rect x="600" y="60" width="180" height="56" rx="12" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="690" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#6FA67B">Dorsal "where / how"</text>
<text x="690" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#6FA67B">location · motion · action</text>
<rect x="600" y="244" width="180" height="56" rx="12" fill="#FBEFD7" stroke="#D7A24C" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="690" y="268" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#9A6A2E">Ventral "what"</text>
<text x="690" y="287" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#9A6A2E">objects · faces · colour</text>
</svg>`,
  receptorTypes:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<rect x="20" y="20" width="350" height="44" rx="10" fill="#ECE9FB"/><rect x="390" y="20" width="350" height="44" rx="10" fill="#E4EDF7"/>
<text x="40" y="48" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4">Ionotropic, fast</text>
<text x="410" y="48" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="800" fill="#6E97C6">Metabotropic, slow</text>
<text x="240" y="48" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">milliseconds</text><text x="640" y="48" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">seconds</text>
<!-- ionotropic: ligand-gated channel -->
<rect x="60" y="120" width="40" height="80" rx="6" fill="#7B6FD4"/><rect x="150" y="120" width="40" height="80" rx="6" fill="#7B6FD4"/>
<rect x="100" y="120" width="50" height="80" fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2"/>
<circle cx="125" cy="100" r="9" fill="#DD8468"/><text x="125" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8" fill="#DD8468">NT</text>
<g fill="#6E97C6"><circle cx="120" cy="150" r="5"/><circle cx="132" cy="170" r="5"/><circle cx="118" cy="188" r="5"/></g>
<path d="M125 210 L125 240" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#rr)"/>
<defs><marker id="rr" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#324A6D"/></marker></defs>
<text x="200" y="160" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D">channel opens,</text><text x="200" y="176" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D">ions flow directly</text>
<text x="125" y="262" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#324A6D">ion flow</text>
<!-- metabotropic: GPCR cascade -->
<rect x="430" y="120" width="40" height="80" rx="14" fill="#6E97C6"/>
<circle cx="450" cy="100" r="9" fill="#DD8468"/><text x="450" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8" fill="#DD8468">NT</text>
<ellipse cx="470" cy="215" rx="22" ry="14" fill="#D7A24C" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="470" y="219" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8" fill="#fff">G-prot</text>
<path d="M450 200 L466 205" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<path d="M492 220 C540 230 560 250 560 270" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#rr)"/>
<text x="520" y="200" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D">triggers a 2nd-messenger</text>
<text x="520" y="216" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D">cascade inside the cell</text>
<text x="560" y="290" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#324A6D">modulates the neuron</text>
</svg>`,
  homunculus:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<text x="380" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">The cortical "homunculus", body map by brain space</text>
<path d="M120 70 C300 50 460 50 640 70" fill="none" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 5"/>
<text x="120" y="64" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#7B6FD4">motor cortex (M1)  ·  central sulcus  ·  somatosensory (S1)</text>
<!-- body parts sized by representation, along the strip -->
<g font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D" text-anchor="middle">
<circle cx="170" cy="120" r="8" fill="#6E97C6"/><text x="170" y="150">toes</text>
<circle cx="240" cy="115" r="10" fill="#6E97C6"/><text x="240" y="150">leg</text>
<circle cx="310" cy="112" r="11" fill="#6E97C6"/><text x="310" y="150">trunk</text>
<circle cx="375" cy="110" r="14" fill="#7B6FD4"/><text x="375" y="155">arm</text>
<circle cx="445" cy="108" r="30" fill="#DD8468"/><text x="445" y="160">HAND</text>
<circle cx="525" cy="110" r="22" fill="#DD8468"/><text x="525" y="155">face</text>
<circle cx="595" cy="115" r="26" fill="#D7A24C"/><text x="595" y="160">LIPS</text>
<circle cx="665" cy="120" r="18" fill="#D7A24C"/><text x="665" y="152">tongue</text>
</g>
<text x="380" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#5E6E88">Hands, lips and face get huge areas, a paper-cut on a fingertip</text>
<text x="380" y="272" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" fill="#5E6E88">screams louder than one on your back, because far more cortex is listening.</text>
</svg>`,
  memorySystems:`<svg viewBox="0 0 800 330" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#324A6D"/></marker></defs>
<rect x="20" y="70" width="150" height="72" rx="13" fill="#ECE9FB" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2"/><text x="95.0" y="103.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="15" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Sensory</text><text x="95.0" y="121.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D" opacity="0.9">memory</text>
<rect x="300" y="70" width="180" height="72" rx="13" fill="#E4EDF7" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="390.0" y="103.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Working / STM</text><text x="390.0" y="121.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D" opacity="0.9">~7 items, seconds</text>
<rect x="610" y="50" width="170" height="120" rx="13" fill="#7B6FD4" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="2"/><text x="695.0" y="107.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="16" font-weight="800" fill="#fff">Long-term</text><text x="695.0" y="125.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#fff" opacity="0.9">memory</text>
<path d="M170 106 H300" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="235" y="98" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">attention</text>
<path d="M480 106 H610" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2.5" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="545" y="98" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">encoding</text>
<path d="M610 130 H490" stroke="#9BA6BA" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 4" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="545" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#9BA6BA">retrieval</text>
<path d="M390 70 C360 40 420 40 470 56" fill="none" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="415" y="38" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#6E97C6">rehearsal</text>
<path d="M95 142 V185" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 4" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="95" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#DD8468">decays fast</text>
<rect x="560" y="230" width="120" height="64" rx="13" fill="#E4F0E6" stroke="#6FA67B" stroke-width="2"/><text x="620.0" y="259.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Declarative</text><text x="620.0" y="277.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D" opacity="0.9">facts · events</text>
<rect x="700" y="230" width="90" height="64" rx="13" fill="#FBEFD7" stroke="#D7A24C" stroke-width="2"/><text x="745.0" y="259.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#324A6D">Procedural</text><text x="745.0" y="277.0" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#324A6D" opacity="0.9">skills</text>
<path d="M660 170 L620 230" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<path d="M720 170 L740 230" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/>
<text x="640" y="312" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#6FA67B">"knowing that"</text>
<text x="745" y="312" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#9A6A2E">"knowing how"</text>
</svg>`,
  emotionTheories:`<svg viewBox="0 0 800 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z" fill="#324A6D"/></marker></defs>
<text x="20" y="24" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4">James–Lange</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="70" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="55.0" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22">🐻</text><path d="M90 72 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="116" y="50" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="191.0" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D">body reacts</text><text x="191.0" y="86" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88">heart races</text><path d="M266 72 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="292" y="50" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#7B6FD4" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="367.0" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D">emotion</text><text x="367.0" y="86" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88">feels afraid</text>
<text x="20" y="144" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#DD8468">Cannon–Bard</text><rect x="20" y="170" width="70" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="55.0" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22">🐻</text><path d="M90 192 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="116" y="170" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="191.0" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D">body + emotion</text><text x="191.0" y="206" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88">at the same time</text><path d="M266 192 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="292" y="170" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#DD8468" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="367.0" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D"></text>
<text x="20" y="264" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="13" font-weight="800" fill="#6E97C6">Schachter–Singer</text><rect x="20" y="290" width="70" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="55.0" y="320" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22">🐻</text><path d="M90 312 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="116" y="290" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="191.0" y="310" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D">arousal + label</text><text x="191.0" y="326" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88">two-factor</text><path d="M266 312 h26" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><rect x="292" y="290" width="150" height="44" rx="10" fill="#fff" stroke="#6E97C6" stroke-width="1.8"/><text x="367.0" y="310" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="12" font-weight="700" fill="#324A6D">emotion</text><text x="367.0" y="326" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88">labelled</text>
</svg>`,
  sleepStages:`<svg viewBox="0 0 800 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="110" y1="40" x2="110" y2="250" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>

<text x="100" y="64">Awake</text><text x="100" y="104">REM</text><text x="100" y="144">N1</text><text x="100" y="184">N2</text><text x="100" y="224">N3 deep</text></g>
<g stroke="#EBE3EC" stroke-width="1"><line x1="110" y1="100" x2="760" y2="100"/><line x1="110" y1="140" x2="760" y2="140"/><line x1="110" y1="180" x2="760" y2="180"/><line x1="110" y1="220" x2="760" y2="220"/></g>

<text x="430" y="285" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="10" fill="#5E6E88">one night · ~90-minute cycles · deep sleep early, REM-rich toward morning</text>

  languageNetwork:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 420" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">

<!-- Broca -->

<!-- Wernicke -->

<text x="470" y="282" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#fff">comprehension</text>
<!-- arcuate fasciculus -->

  imagingMethods:`<svg viewBox="0 0 760 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<line x1="90" y1="40" x2="90" y2="320" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<line x1="90" y1="320" x2="720" y2="320" stroke="#324A6D" stroke-width="2"/>
<text x="60" y="180" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="11" fill="#5E6E88" transform="rotate(-90 40 180)">spatial detail →</text>

<circle cx="560" cy="250" r="13" fill="#6E97C6"/><text x="430" y="248" fill="#6E97C6" text-anchor="end">EEG</text><text x="430" y="263" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="end">millisecond timing, coarse</text>
<circle cx="600" cy="210" r="13" fill="#D7A24C"/><text x="470" y="208" fill="#9A6A2E" text-anchor="end">MEG</text>
<circle cx="630" cy="90" r="13" fill="#6FA67B"/><text x="500" y="88" fill="#6FA67B" text-anchor="end">single-unit</text><text x="500" y="103" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="8.5" fill="#5E6E88" text-anchor="end">one neuron · invasive</text>
</g>
<text x="120" y="300" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9.5" fill="#9BA6BA">lesion studies: precise but rely on accidents of damage</text>
</svg>`,
  biopsychosocial:`<svg viewBox="0 0 700 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
<polygon points="350,60 120,290 580,290" fill="none" stroke="#EBE3EC" stroke-width="2"/>

<text x="350" y="66" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Hanken Grotesk',sans-serif" font-size="14" font-weight="800" fill="#7B6FD4">Biological</text>
<text x="350" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-family="'Space Mono',monospace" font-size="9" fill="#5E6E88">genes · chemistry</text>

};
const LESSONS=[
{id:0,c:"var(--violet)",num:"Lesson 01",title:"The Nervous System",sub:"The body's communication network, from spinal reflexes to the fight-or-flight switch and the stress axis.",lead:"Before opening up the brain, we need the wiring diagram of the whole body. The nervous system is the high-speed communication network that lets you sense the world, integrate that information, decide, and act, sometimes within a few thousandths of a second. It is built from two cell families and organised into nested divisions, each with a distinct job.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">1.1</span>Two cell families: neurons and glia</h2>
 <p>The nervous system runs on two kinds of cell. <b>Neurons</b> are the signalling cells, they generate and transmit the electrochemical impulses that carry information. <b>Glia</b> ("glue") were once thought to be mere scaffolding, but we now know they are active partners: they insulate axons, feed neurons, prune synapses, defend against infection, and shape signalling. You have roughly 86 billion neurons and a comparable number of glia.</p>
 <p>We dissect the neuron in detail in Lesson 4. For now, the key point is that <b>information = patterns of neural firing</b>, and everything below is about how those signals are organised and routed.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">1.2</span>Central vs peripheral: the master division</h2>
 <p>Every neuron belongs to one of two systems. The <b>central nervous system (CNS)</b>, brain and spinal cord, is the command centre where information is integrated and decisions are made. The <b>peripheral nervous system (PNS)</b> is the vast web of nerves connecting that command centre to every muscle, gland and patch of skin.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 1.1 · Organisation of the nervous system</div>${DIAG.nervousTree}<div class="figcap">The nervous system divides into central and peripheral. The peripheral system splits into somatic (voluntary) and autonomic (automatic) branches, and the autonomic branch into its two opposing modes, sympathetic and parasympathetic.</div></div>
 <p>The traffic on these nerves runs two ways. <b>Afferent (sensory) neurons</b> carry information <i>toward</i> the CNS; <b>efferent (motor) neurons</b> carry commands <i>away</i> from it to muscles and glands. The memory aid: <b>A</b>fferent = <b>A</b>rriving, <b>E</b>fferent = <b>E</b>xiting.</p>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Division</th><th>Includes</th><th>Core job</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Central (CNS)</td><td>Brain &amp; spinal cord</td><td>Integrate information, issue commands</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Peripheral, somatic</td><td>Sensory + motor nerves to skin &amp; skeletal muscle</td><td>Voluntary movement &amp; conscious sensation</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Peripheral, autonomic</td><td>Nerves to organs, glands, smooth muscle</td><td>Automatic regulation of internal state</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">1.3</span>The autonomic nervous system in depth</h2>
 <p>The autonomic system runs the processes you never consciously command: heartbeat, digestion, pupil size, breathing rate, blood pressure, sweating. It has two branches that act in opposition, like an accelerator and a brake on the same engine, and most organs receive both.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 1.2 · The autonomic balance</div>${DIAG.autonomic}<div class="figcap">The sympathetic branch mobilises the body for action; the parasympathetic branch restores and conserves. Your moment-to-moment physical state reflects which branch is currently dominant.</div></div>
 <ul>
 <li><b>Sympathetic, "fight or flight."</b> Activated by threat or effort. It releases <b>norepinephrine</b> at its targets and triggers the adrenal glands to flood the blood with <b>adrenaline</b>: heart rate and force rise, airways widen, pupils dilate, digestion pauses, and stored glucose is released for fast fuel.</li>
 <li><b>Parasympathetic, "rest and digest."</b> Dominant in calm states. It works largely through the <b>vagus nerve</b>, releasing <b>acetylcholine</b> to slow the heart, constrict pupils, and ramp digestion back up, rebuilding energy reserves.</li>
 </ul>
 <div class="callout key"><div class="ct">🔑 Key idea</div><p>The two branches are almost never fully "off." They are in constant tug-of-war, and your heart rate, digestion and alertness at any instant reflect their balance. The relative dominance of the calming vagal branch (often measured as <b>heart-rate variability</b>) is a useful index of physiological resilience.</p></div>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · the enteric nervous system</div><p>The gut contains its own mesh of some 500 million neurons, the <b>enteric nervous system</b>, sometimes called the "second brain." It can run digestion autonomously and communicates constantly with the brain via the vagus nerve (the <b>gut–brain axis</b>), which is why stress upsets the stomach and gut states influence mood.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">1.4</span>The spinal cord and the reflex arc</h2>
 <p>The spinal cord relays messages between brain and body, but it can also act on its own. A <b>reflex arc</b>, like yanking your hand off a hot stove, is processed in the cord <i>before</i> the signal reaches the brain. A sensory neuron carries the alarm to the cord, an interneuron passes it straight to a motor neuron, and the muscle contracts. You move first and consciously feel the pain a fraction of a second later.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 1.3 · A spinal reflex arc</div>${DIAG.reflexArc}<div class="figcap">Pain travels in on a sensory neuron, is relayed by an interneuron in the cord, and travels out on a motor neuron, pulling the hand away before the brain registers pain. Routing protective responses through the cord saves 100+ milliseconds.</div></div>

 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure · The stress response</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="The stress response: alarm, resistance, exhaustion"><div class="figcap">A perceived threat activates the amygdala and the stress response. Short bursts mobilise the body; prolonged stress wears it down.</div></div><h2><span class="num">1.5</span>The stress response and the HPA axis</h2>
 <p>Threat triggers two waves. The fast wave is the sympathetic adrenaline surge (seconds). The slower, sustained wave is the <b>HPA axis</b>: the <b>h</b>ypothalamus releases CRH, prompting the <b>p</b>ituitary to release ACTH, which drives the <b>a</b>drenal glands to release <b>cortisol</b>. Cortisol mobilises energy and sharpens short-term focus, adaptive in a crisis.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>When stress is chronic, the system never resets. Persistently high cortisol contributes to hypertension, suppressed immunity, disrupted sleep, metabolic problems, and, because the hippocampus is rich in cortisol receptors, memory impairment. Spinal cord injuries cut communication below the lesion; the higher the injury, the more is lost, and cervical injuries can impair breathing. In <b>autonomic dysreflexia</b>, a damaged cord produces dangerous uncontrolled sympathetic surges.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Which two organs make up the central nervous system?", "opts": ["Heart and lungs", "Brain and spinal cord", "Brain and peripheral nerves", "Spinal cord and adrenal glands"], "a": 1, "why": "The CNS is precisely the brain plus the spinal cord; everything else is peripheral."}, {"q": "You are startled by a bang and your heart races. Which branch is dominant?", "opts": ["Parasympathetic", "Somatic", "Sympathetic", "Enteric"], "a": 2, "why": "A racing heart and adrenaline surge are the signature of the sympathetic 'fight-or-flight' response."}, {"q": "In the HPA axis, which hormone is ultimately released by the adrenal glands?", "opts": ["Dopamine", "Cortisol", "Acetylcholine", "Insulin"], "a": 1, "why": "Hypothalamus→pituitary→adrenal: the adrenals release cortisol, the main stress hormone."}, {"q": "A reflex like pulling your hand off a stove is processed mainly by the…", "opts": ["Frontal lobe", "Spinal cord", "Cerebellum", "Hypothalamus"], "a": 1, "why": "Reflex arcs are handled in the spinal cord so the body reacts before the brain registers pain."}, {"q": "Afferent neurons carry information…", "opts": ["Away from the CNS to muscles", "Toward the CNS (sensory)", "Only within the brain", "Between the two hemispheres"], "a": 1, "why": "Afferent = arriving: sensory neurons carry information toward the CNS."}]},
{id:1,c:"var(--coral)",num:"Lesson 02",title:"Brain Anatomy: The Four Lobes",sub:"A region-by-region tour of the cerebral cortex, each lobe, its key sub-areas, and what goes wrong when they're damaged.",lead:"The cerebral cortex is the brain's outer sheet of tissue, folded to pack roughly a square metre of neurons into the skull. It is divided into four lobes, each housing specialised regions, yet all in constant conversation across the two hemispheres. We take each lobe in turn, in depth.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">2.1</span>Orientation: gyri, sulci and landmarks</h2>
 <p>The cortex's raised ridges are <b>gyri</b>, the grooves <b>sulci</b>, and the deepest grooves <b>fissures</b>. Two landmark sulci define the lobe boundaries: the <b>central sulcus</b> separates the frontal lobe (front) from the parietal lobe (behind it), and the deep <b>lateral (Sylvian) fissure</b> marks off the temporal lobe below. The cortex is organised <b>contralaterally</b>, the left hemisphere controls and senses the right side of the body, and vice versa.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 2.1 · The four lobes (lateral view)</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="Labelled lateral view of the brain showing the four lobes, cerebellum and brainstem"><div class="figcap">A side view of the left hemisphere. The central sulcus divides frontal from parietal; the lateral fissure sets off the temporal lobe. The cerebellum and brainstem sit beneath and behind. Colours mark the four lobes.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">2.2</span>The frontal lobe, the executive</h2>
 <p>The largest lobe and the brain's chief executive. It contains, from front to back:</p>
 <div class="termgrid">
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Prefrontal cortex</div><div class="td">Planning, judgement, working memory, impulse control, personality. Subdivides into dorsolateral (planning/working memory), orbitofrontal (reward, social restraint), and ventromedial (emotion-guided decisions).</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Premotor &amp; SMA</div><div class="td">Prepare and sequence movements before they execute.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Primary motor cortex (M1)</div><div class="td">A strip along the central sulcus that commands voluntary movement, mapped as a motor homunculus.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Broca's area</div><div class="td">Usually left-hemisphere; assembles fluent, grammatical speech.</div></div>
 </div>
 <p>The prefrontal cortex is the <b>last region to fully mature</b>, around age 25, central to understanding adolescent impulsivity (Lesson 6).</p>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Classic case · Phineas Gage, 1848</div><p>An iron rod blasted through Gage's frontal lobe. He survived, but the steady foreman became impulsive, profane and unreliable, "no longer Gage." It was the first hard evidence that the frontal lobe governs personality, planning and social restraint. Damage here produces <b>dysexecutive syndrome</b>: intact IQ but impaired planning, inhibition and judgement.</p></div>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Broca's-area damage causes <b>Broca's (expressive) aphasia</b>: halting, effortful, telegraphic speech with largely preserved comprehension. Orbitofrontal damage can cause disinhibition; dorsolateral damage impairs working memory and flexible planning.</p></div>

 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure · The prefrontal cortex, your executive</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="Functions of the prefrontal cortex"><div class="figcap">The prefrontal cortex plans, decides, stays focused, controls impulses and thinks flexibly, and is still maturing into the mid-20s.</div></div><h2><span class="num">2.3</span>The parietal lobe, the body map and spatial sense</h2>
 <p>Just behind the central sulcus sits the <b>primary somatosensory cortex (S1)</b>, which receives touch, temperature, pain and body position. It is laid out as the <b>somatosensory homunculus</b>, a body map in which the most sensitive parts claim wildly disproportionate territory.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 2.2 · The cortical homunculus</div>${DIAG.homunculus}<div class="figcap">Body parts drawn in proportion to the cortex devoted to them. Hands, lips and face dominate, which is why a fingertip is far more sensitive and dexterous than your back. The motor strip (M1) has its own parallel map.</div></div>
 <p>The <b>posterior parietal cortex</b> integrates vision and touch to locate your body and objects in space, direct attention, and guide reaching, the start of the dorsal "where/how" visual stream. The <b>angular and supramarginal gyri</b> contribute to reading, arithmetic and language.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Right parietal damage causes <b>hemispatial neglect</b>: patients ignore the left side of space, eating from one half of a plate or drawing a clock with all numbers crammed right. Left parietal damage can cause <b>Gerstmann syndrome</b> (agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, left–right confusion). Damage may also cause <b>apraxia</b>, the inability to perform learned movements on command despite intact strength.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">2.4</span>The temporal lobe, sound, language and memory</h2>
 <p>Below the lateral fissure, the temporal lobe houses the <b>primary auditory cortex (A1)</b> for sound, and (usually on the left) <b>Wernicke's area</b> for language <i>comprehension</i>. Its underside holds the <b>fusiform face area</b>, specialised for recognising faces, and forms the endpoint of the ventral "what" stream. Buried within the medial temporal lobe are the <b>hippocampus</b> and <b>amygdala</b> (Lesson 3).</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Wernicke's-area damage causes <b>Wernicke's (receptive) aphasia</b>: fluent but meaningless speech and poor comprehension, the mirror image of Broca's aphasia. Damage to the fusiform face area causes <b>prosopagnosia</b> ("face blindness"), an inability to recognise familiar faces despite normal vision.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">2.5</span>The occipital lobe, vision, in depth</h2>
 <p>The occipital lobe is devoted almost entirely to vision. Signals travel a precise route: from the retina along the optic nerves, crossing at the <b>optic chiasm</b>, relayed by the <b>lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)</b> of the thalamus, and arriving at the <b>primary visual cortex (V1)</b>, also called striate cortex.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 2.3 · The visual pathway and two streams</div>${DIAG.visualPathway}<div class="figcap">From eyes to V1 via the optic chiasm and the thalamic LGN. From V1, processing splits into a dorsal 'where/how' stream (toward the parietal lobe) and a ventral 'what' stream (toward the temporal lobe).</div></div>
 <p>V1 holds a <b>retinotopic map</b>, neighbouring points in the visual field map to neighbouring patches of cortex. From V1, signals pass through a hierarchy of areas (V2–V5) that extract progressively more complex features, then split into two great streams:</p>
 <ul>
 <li><b>Dorsal "where / how" stream</b> (toward parietal): location, motion, and spatial guidance of action. Includes area <b>V5/MT</b>, specialised for motion.</li>
 <li><b>Ventral "what" stream</b> (toward temporal): object identity, faces, and colour. Includes area <b>V4</b>, important for colour.</li>
 </ul>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance · when vision breaks</div><p>Destroying V1 causes <b>cortical blindness</b>, yet some patients show <b>blindsight</b>, accurately guessing or reaching for objects they report not seeing, because older subcortical visual routes survive. Other selective deficits map onto the hierarchy: <b>visual agnosia</b> (seeing but not recognising objects), <b>achromatopsia</b> (loss of colour perception, V4), and <b>akinetopsia</b> (loss of motion perception, V5), the patient sees the world as a series of frozen snapshots.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">2.6</span>Two hemispheres, one mind</h2>
 <p>The hemispheres are joined by the <b>corpus callosum</b>, a thick band of ~200 million fibres. They are not identical: in most people the left is dominant for language and detail, the right for spatial processing, faces and global patterns, a tendency called <b>lateralisation</b> (often overstated in pop culture).</p>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Classic case · split-brain studies (Sperry &amp; Gazzaniga)</div><p>In patients whose corpus callosum was severed to control epilepsy, the hemispheres could be tested separately. An object shown only to the right hemisphere could be picked out by the left hand but <i>not named</i>, because language sat in the disconnected left hemisphere. The studies, which earned a Nobel Prize, revealed how the two hemispheres specialise, and normally collaborate seamlessly through the callosum.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Which lobe contains the primary visual cortex (V1)?", "opts": ["Frontal", "Temporal", "Occipital", "Parietal"], "a": 2, "why": "V1, the primary visual cortex, sits in the occipital lobe."}, {"q": "A patient sees normally but cannot recognise familiar faces. This is…", "opts": ["Aphasia", "Prosopagnosia", "Neglect", "Akinetopsia"], "a": 1, "why": "Prosopagnosia (face blindness) follows damage to the fusiform face area in the temporal lobe."}, {"q": "The dorsal visual stream is best summarised as the…", "opts": ["'What' pathway", "'Where / how' pathway", "Auditory pathway", "Language pathway"], "a": 1, "why": "The dorsal stream toward the parietal lobe handles location, motion and the spatial guidance of action."}, {"q": "Fluent but meaningless speech with poor comprehension indicates damage to…", "opts": ["Broca's area", "Wernicke's area", "M1", "The cerebellum"], "a": 1, "why": "Wernicke's (receptive) aphasia is fluent but empty speech with impaired understanding."}, {"q": "In split-brain patients, an object shown only to the right hemisphere cannot be named because…", "opts": ["The right hemisphere is blind", "Language is usually in the left hemisphere", "The object is too small", "The thalamus is severed"], "a": 1, "why": "Language is typically left-lateralised, so the disconnected right hemisphere can't name what it sees."}]},
{id:2,c:"var(--teal)",num:"Lesson 03",title:"Subcortical Structures",sub:"The deep machinery beneath the cortex, taking each structure in turn, in depth.",lead:"Tucked under the wrinkled cortex are evolutionarily older structures that handle emotion, memory, movement, drive and the basic business of staying alive. Many form the limbic system, the emotional and memory core. We examine all seven in depth.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">3.1</span>The deep structures at a glance</h2>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 3.1 · The brain from the inside (medial view)</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="The brain's control centre: prefrontal cortex, amygdala, hippocampus, hypothalamus and pituitary"><div class="figcap">A cutaway down the midline reveals the deep structures: the thalamus relays the senses, the hypothalamus keeps the body in balance, the amygdala and hippocampus handle emotion and memory, the basal ganglia shape movement, and the cerebellum and brainstem sit at the back and base.</div></div>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Structure</th><th>Core role</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Thalamus</td><td>Sensory relay (all senses except smell); arousal</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Hypothalamus</td><td>Homeostasis: hunger, thirst, temperature, hormones</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Amygdala</td><td>Threat detection, fear, emotional memory</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Hippocampus</td><td>Forming new memories, spatial navigation</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Basal ganglia</td><td>Action selection, habits, reward learning</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Cerebellum</td><td>Balance, coordination, motor timing</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Brainstem</td><td>Breathing, heart rate, consciousness</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">3.2</span>The thalamus, the grand relay</h2>
 <p>Sitting at the centre of the brain, the thalamus is the gateway through which nearly all sensory information passes on its way to the cortex, vision, hearing, touch and taste, but <b>not smell</b>, which has a direct route. It is not a passive switchboard: dedicated nuclei relay each sense (the <b>LGN</b> for vision, the <b>MGN</b> for hearing, the <b>VPL/VPM</b> for body sensation) and motor nuclei (VA/VL) feed the cortex from the cerebellum and basal ganglia. The thalamus also gates what reaches awareness and, with the brainstem, regulates sleep and arousal.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Thalamic strokes can cause profound sensory loss or relentless <b>central (thalamic) pain</b>. The rare prion disease <b>fatal familial insomnia</b> destroys thalamic nuclei and robs patients of the ability to sleep at all, invariably fatally, underscoring the thalamus's role in consciousness.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">3.3</span>The hypothalamus, the body's thermostat</h2>
 <p>Barely four grams, the hypothalamus is the master regulator of <b>homeostasis</b>, keeping the body's internal conditions within survivable bounds. It governs the "four F's": feeding, fighting, fleeing and reproduction. Distinct nuclei control hunger (the lateral hypothalamus drives eating; the ventromedial signals satiety), thirst, and body temperature. Its <b>suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN)</b> is the body's master clock, syncing circadian rhythms to light. And it commands the entire hormonal system by controlling the <b>pituitary gland</b>, including the HPA stress axis from Lesson 1.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">3.4</span>The amygdala, the alarm</h2>
 <p>This almond-shaped structure fires a threat response in under 200 milliseconds, before you consciously know what scared you. Joseph LeDoux mapped two routes for fear: a fast, crude <b>"low road"</b> (thalamus straight to amygdala) that reacts first, and a slower, accurate <b>"high road"</b> through the cortex that fills in detail. This is why you flinch at a curved stick before the slow road confirms it isn't a snake. The amygdala also <b>tags memories with emotion</b>, which is why frightening or thrilling events are remembered so vividly.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>In the rare <b>Urbach–Wiethe disease</b>, which destroys the amygdala, patients lose the ability to feel or recognise fear. Amygdala hyperactivity is implicated in anxiety disorders and PTSD, where the threat system becomes overtuned, and hard to "think away," because the fast low road bypasses conscious reasoning.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">3.5</span>The hippocampus, the scribe and the map</h2>
 <p>The hippocampus is essential for forming new <b>declarative</b> memories (facts and events) and for <b>spatial navigation</b>. It does not store memories permanently; it consolidates them and gradually hands them to the cortex over time. John O'Keefe's discovery of <b>place cells</b>, neurons that fire only when you're in a specific location, showed the hippocampus builds an internal map of space, work that won a Nobel Prize.</p>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Classic case · Patient H.M., 1953</div><p>To treat epilepsy, surgeons removed Henry Molaison's hippocampi on both sides. The seizures eased, but he could never form a new conscious memory again. Every introduction was brand new for the rest of his life. Yet he could still <i>learn new motor skills</i> (improving at mirror-drawing he had no memory of practising). H.M. proved memory isn't one system, and that the hippocampus is the gateway for new declarative memories specifically.</p></div>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · stress, neurogenesis and Alzheimer's</div><p>The hippocampus is one of the few regions where new neurons are born in adulthood (<b>neurogenesis</b>). Chronic stress and high cortisol damage it, contributing to the memory problems of depression and PTSD, while exercise and some antidepressants boost neurogenesis. The hippocampus is also among the first regions to degenerate in <b>Alzheimer's disease</b>, which is why early memory loss is the hallmark symptom.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">3.6</span>The basal ganglia, action selection and habit</h2>
 <p>A cluster of structures (the <b>striatum</b>, caudate and putamen, plus the globus pallidus, subthalamic nucleus and substantia nigra) that act as the brain's gatekeeper for movement and habit. Through competing <b>direct</b> ("go") and <b>indirect</b> ("stop") pathways, they select which actions to release and which to suppress. They are also central to <b>habit formation</b> and, fed by dopamine, to <b>reward-based learning</b>.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Loss of dopamine input from the <b>substantia nigra</b> causes <b>Parkinson's disease</b> (tremor, rigidity, slowed movement). Degeneration of the striatum causes <b>Huntington's disease</b> (involuntary movements). Basal-ganglia circuits are also implicated in <b>obsessive–compulsive disorder</b> and <b>Tourette syndrome</b>.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">3.7</span>The cerebellum, the surprising heavyweight</h2>
 <p>Though only ~10% of the brain's volume, the cerebellum ("little brain") holds roughly <b>80% of all your neurons</b>. It constantly compares the movement you <i>intended</i> with the movement you actually <i>made</i> and corrects the error, making it essential for smooth, coordinated, well-timed action and for motor learning. Increasingly it's recognised to contribute to cognition and emotion too (the <b>cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome</b>).</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Cerebellar damage causes <b>ataxia</b>: clumsy, uncoordinated movement, tremor and unsteady gait. Alcohol depresses the cerebellum, which is why intoxication wrecks balance and coordination and why roadside sobriety tests probe exactly these functions.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">3.8</span>The brainstem, the keeper of life</h2>
 <p>The brainstem (midbrain, pons and medulla) runs the non-negotiables. The <b>medulla</b> controls breathing, heart rate and blood pressure. The <b>pons</b> relays signals and participates in sleep and breathing. The <b>midbrain</b> handles reflexive eye and head movements and contains dopamine centres. Threading through it all is the <b>reticular formation</b>, whose ascending activating system governs arousal, wakefulness and consciousness itself.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Because the brainstem runs breathing and circulation, <b>brainstem death</b> is the clinical and legal definition of death. Damage to the pons can cause <b>locked-in syndrome</b>: full awareness but near-total paralysis, sometimes leaving only vertical eye movements as a channel to the world.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Which structure is the brain's rapid threat-detection 'alarm'?", "opts": ["Hippocampus", "Amygdala", "Thalamus", "Cerebellum"], "a": 1, "why": "The amygdala triggers fear responses faster than conscious awareness."}, {"q": "Nearly all sensory information reaches the cortex via the thalamus, except for which sense?", "opts": ["Vision", "Hearing", "Smell", "Touch"], "a": 2, "why": "Smell bypasses the thalamus and projects more directly; the other senses are relayed through it."}, {"q": "Loss of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra (basal ganglia) causes…", "opts": ["Alzheimer's disease", "Parkinson's disease", "Wernicke's aphasia", "Ataxia"], "a": 1, "why": "Nigrostriatal dopamine loss produces Parkinson's tremor, rigidity and slowed movement."}, {"q": "Patient H.M. could still learn new motor skills, showing that the hippocampus is needed mainly for…", "opts": ["All memory", "New declarative (fact/event) memory", "Motor habits", "Vision"], "a": 1, "why": "H.M. proved the hippocampus is the gateway for new declarative memories, not motor learning."}, {"q": "Which structure contains about 80% of the brain's neurons and coordinates movement?", "opts": ["Thalamus", "Cerebellum", "Hypothalamus", "Amygdala"], "a": 1, "why": "Despite its small size, the cerebellum holds the majority of neurons and governs coordination and timing."}, {"q": "Damage to which structure most directly threatens breathing and heart rate?", "opts": ["Cerebellum", "Brainstem", "Corpus callosum", "Hippocampus"], "a": 1, "why": "The brainstem (medulla) runs the vital cardiorespiratory functions."}]},
{id:3,c:"var(--green)",num:"Lesson 04",title:"Neurons & the Action Potential",sub:"The brain's basic unit, the supporting glia, and the electrochemical spark that carries every thought.",lead:"You have around 86 billion neurons, each a tiny electrochemical machine, supported by an equal number of glial cells. Understanding a single neuron is the key to the whole brain, because the brain is, at bottom, neurons talking to each other billions of times a second.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">4.1</span>Anatomy of a neuron</h2>
 <p>Neurons come in many shapes, but most share the same functional parts, arranged so information flows one way: in through the dendrites, integrated in the soma, out along the axon, across the synapse to the next cell.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 4.1 · The structure of a neuron</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="Diagram of a neuron: dendrites, soma, axon, myelin sheath and synapse"><div class="figcap">Signals arrive on the dendrites, are summed in the soma, and travel out along the axon. Myelin segments insulate the axon and speed the signal, which jumps the gaps (nodes of Ranvier) before reaching the terminals and the synapse.</div></div>
 <div class="termgrid">
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Dendrites</div><div class="td">Branching antennae that receive signals from thousands of other neurons.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Soma (cell body)</div><div class="td">Houses the nucleus; integrates all incoming excitation and inhibition.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Axon hillock</div><div class="td">The decision point where the soma's summed charge is compared to threshold.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Axon</div><div class="td">The cable carrying the outgoing signal away from the soma.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Myelin sheath</div><div class="td">Fatty insulation that dramatically speeds the signal.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Axon terminals</div><div class="td">Branched ends that release chemical messengers at the synapse.</div></div>
 </div>
 <p>Neurons are often classified by job, <b>sensory</b> (afferent), <b>motor</b> (efferent), and <b>interneurons</b> (connecting the two, the vast majority), and by shape (multipolar, bipolar, unipolar).</p>

 <h2><span class="num">4.2</span>The glia, the essential support cast</h2>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Glial cell</th><th>Job</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Oligodendrocytes (CNS) / Schwann cells (PNS)</td><td>Form the myelin that insulates axons</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Astrocytes</td><td>Feed neurons, regulate the chemical environment, form the blood–brain barrier, modulate synapses</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Microglia</td><td>The brain's immune cells, clear debris and prune unused synapses</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Ependymal cells</td><td>Produce and circulate cerebrospinal fluid</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">4.3</span>The resting potential</h2>
 <p>A neuron at rest is <b>negatively charged inside</b> relative to outside, its <b>resting potential</b> is about −70 millivolts. This charge is maintained by the <b>sodium–potassium pump</b>, which pushes three Na⁺ ions out for every two K⁺ it lets in, and by the membrane's selective leakiness to potassium. The result is two gradients, chemical (concentration) and electrical, poised like a loaded spring, ready to drive ions when channels open.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">4.4</span>From graded potentials to threshold</h2>
 <p>Incoming signals produce small, local voltage changes called <b>graded potentials</b>: excitatory inputs (<b>EPSPs</b>) nudge the cell toward firing, inhibitory inputs (<b>IPSPs</b>) push it away. The soma <b>sums</b> these inputs across space (many synapses at once, <b>spatial summation</b>) and time (rapid repeats, <b>temporal summation</b>). If the total at the axon hillock reaches the <b>threshold</b> of about −55 mV, an action potential fires.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">4.5</span>The action potential, step by step</h2>
 <p>At threshold, voltage-gated <b>sodium channels</b> fly open and Na⁺ floods in, flipping the inside briefly positive to about +40 mV (<b>depolarisation</b>). The Na⁺ channels then close and voltage-gated <b>potassium channels</b> open, letting K⁺ out to restore the negative charge (<b>repolarisation</b>), briefly overshooting below rest (<b>hyperpolarisation</b>) before settling back.</p>
