 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 4.2 · One action potential</div>${DIAG.actionPotential}<div class="figcap">From rest (−70 mV), excitation reaches threshold (−55 mV); sodium rushes in (the spike to +40 mV), then potassium flows out to restore the charge, overshooting before returning to rest.</div></div>
 <div class="callout key"><div class="ct">🔑 All-or-nothing &amp; refractory periods</div><p>An action potential either fires fully or not at all, there is no half-spike. Intensity is coded by <b>firing rate</b>, not spike size. Immediately afterward, the <b>absolute refractory period</b> prevents any new spike (Na⁺ channels are inactivated), and the following <b>relative refractory period</b> requires a stronger-than-usual stimulus. Together these cap firing rate and force the signal to travel one way down the axon.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">4.6</span>Propagation and the role of myelin</h2>
 <p>The spike regenerates itself down the axon. Where the axon is myelinated, the signal "jumps" between the bare gaps, the <b>nodes of Ranvier</b>, in <b>saltatory conduction</b>, boosting speed from about 1 m/s on a bare axon to over <b>100 m/s</b>. Conduction velocity rises with both myelination and axon diameter.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>In <b>multiple sclerosis</b>, the immune system attacks CNS myelin, slowing or blocking signals and causing weakness, numbness and visual problems. <b>ALS</b> destroys motor neurons. Local anaesthetics work by blocking Na⁺ channels so the spike can't fire; the pufferfish toxin <b>tetrodotoxin</b> does the same, lethally.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">4.7</span>Crossing the synapse</h2>
 <p>When the spike reaches the axon terminal, the electrical signal becomes <b>chemical</b>. Depolarisation opens voltage-gated <b>calcium channels</b>; the Ca²⁺ influx triggers neurotransmitter-filled vesicles to fuse with the membrane and release their contents into the synaptic gap. The molecules drift across and bind receptors on the next neuron, generating new EPSPs or IPSPs. The receiving neuron sums these and, if threshold is crossed, fires its own spike. Most synapses are <b>chemical</b>; a minority are <b>electrical</b> (gap junctions) and far faster but less flexible. We follow the chemistry in Lesson 5.</p>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Which part of the neuron receives incoming signals?", "opts": ["Axon", "Dendrites", "Myelin", "Synapse"], "a": 1, "why": "Dendrites are the branching receivers collecting signals from other neurons."}, {"q": "Which glial cell produces myelin in the central nervous system?", "opts": ["Astrocyte", "Microglia", "Oligodendrocyte", "Ependymal cell"], "a": 2, "why": "Oligodendrocytes myelinate CNS axons (Schwann cells do so in the PNS)."}, {"q": "The resting potential (~−70 mV) is maintained largely by the…", "opts": ["Calcium pump", "Sodium–potassium pump", "Myelin sheath", "Synaptic vesicles"], "a": 1, "why": "The Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) plus K⁺ leak set the resting potential."}, {"q": "'All-or-nothing' means a stronger stimulus produces…", "opts": ["A bigger spike", "More frequent spikes", "A slower spike", "No spike"], "a": 1, "why": "Spike size is fixed; the brain codes intensity as a higher firing rate."}, {"q": "At the axon terminal, what ion's influx triggers neurotransmitter release?", "opts": ["Potassium (K⁺)", "Calcium (Ca²⁺)", "Chloride (Cl⁻)", "Magnesium"], "a": 1, "why": "Calcium influx through voltage-gated channels triggers vesicle fusion and release."}]},
{id:4,c:"var(--amber)",num:"Lesson 05",title:"Neurotransmitters",sub:"The brain's chemical vocabulary, every major messenger in depth, and how drugs act on each.",lead:"If neurons are the words, neurotransmitters are the meaning. These chemical messengers shape mood, motivation, alertness, movement and learning, and the great majority of psychiatric and neurological drugs work by nudging them. We cover each major transmitter in depth.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">5.1</span>Inside the synapse</h2>
 <p>Neurotransmitters are stored in <b>vesicles</b> in the axon terminal. An arriving action potential (via calcium influx) makes vesicles fuse with the membrane and release their cargo into the <b>synaptic cleft</b>. The molecules cross and bind <b>receptors</b> on the receiving cell in a lock-and-key fit, then are cleared away, broken down by enzymes or vacuumed back up by <b>reuptake</b> transporters for recycling.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 5.1 · A chemical synapse</div>${DIAG.synapse}<div class="figcap">An arriving spike triggers vesicles to release neurotransmitter into the cleft. The molecules bind receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, then are cleared by reuptake. Most drugs act at one of these steps.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.2</span>Two ways a receptor responds</h2>
 <p>Whether a transmitter excites or calms a neuron depends on the <b>receptor</b>, not the chemical alone. Receptors come in two great families:</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 5.2 · Ionotropic vs metabotropic receptors</div>${DIAG.receptorTypes}<div class="figcap">Ionotropic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels, fast, direct, millisecond effects. Metabotropic receptors are G-protein-coupled and trigger slower second-messenger cascades that modulate the cell over seconds. The same transmitter can act on both.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.3</span>Glutamate &amp; GABA, the accelerator and the brake</h2>
 <p>Two amino-acid transmitters do most of the brain's heavy lifting. <b>Glutamate</b> is the main <b>excitatory</b> transmitter, the accelerator. Its <b>NMDA receptor</b> is a coincidence detector central to learning and LTP (Lesson 7). <b>GABA</b> is the main <b>inhibitory</b> transmitter, the brake, opening chloride channels that calm neurons.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 5.3 · Glutamate vs GABA</div>${DIAG.brakeAccel}<div class="figcap">Glutamate drives neurons toward firing; GABA holds them back. Too much excitation tips into seizures; boosting GABA produces calm or sedation.</div></div>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · how these are drugged</div><p><b>Benzodiazepines, barbiturates and alcohol</b> all enhance GABA-A receptor activity, which is why they sedate, reduce anxiety, and (in overdose, especially combined) dangerously suppress breathing. Excess glutamate causes <b>excitotoxicity</b>, a major source of neuron death after stroke. Too little GABA braking (or too much glutamate) can trigger <b>seizures</b>.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.4</span>Dopamine, reward, motivation and movement</h2>
 <p>Dopamine travels four main pathways: the <b>mesolimbic</b> (reward/motivation), <b>mesocortical</b> (cognition/motivation), <b>nigrostriatal</b> (movement), and <b>tuberoinfundibular</b> (hormones). Crucially, dopamine does not simply signal pleasure, it signals <b>reward prediction error</b>: it fires when reward is <i>better than expected</i>, stays quiet when reward matches expectation, and dips when an expected reward fails to arrive. This is how the brain learns what to repeat.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Too little dopamine in the nigrostriatal pathway → <b>Parkinson's disease</b>. Overactivity in mesolimbic dopamine is central to the <b>dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia</b>, and antipsychotics work largely by blocking <b>D2 receptors</b>. Addictive drugs hijack the mesolimbic system, flooding it with dopamine far beyond any natural reward.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.5</span>Serotonin (5-HT), mood, sleep and appetite</h2>
 <p>Produced in the brainstem's <b>raphe nuclei</b> and projected brain-wide, serotonin modulates mood, sleep, appetite, aggression and impulse control. Roughly 90% of the body's serotonin actually sits in the gut, regulating digestion. Low serotonin signalling is linked to depression and anxiety.</p>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · SSRIs and hallucinogens</div><p><b>SSRIs</b> (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) block serotonin's reuptake transporter, leaving more in the cleft, the workhorse antidepressants. Classic <b>psychedelics</b> (LSD, psilocybin) act as agonists at the serotonin <b>5-HT2A</b> receptor.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.6</span>Norepinephrine, alertness and the stress response</h2>
 <p>Made mainly in the brainstem's <b>locus coeruleus</b>, norepinephrine (noradrenaline) drives <b>arousal, vigilance and attention</b>, and is the sympathetic system's frontline transmitter in fight-or-flight. It sharpens focus under stress but, chronically elevated, contributes to anxiety and hypervigilance. <b>SNRIs</b> raise both serotonin and norepinephrine for depression; some ADHD medications act on this system.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">5.7</span>Acetylcholine, movement, attention and memory</h2>
 <p>The first neurotransmitter ever discovered. At the <b>neuromuscular junction</b> it commands muscle contraction (via nicotinic receptors); in the autonomic system it carries parasympathetic signals; and from the basal forebrain it supports <b>attention and memory</b> (via muscarinic receptors).</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Degeneration of acetylcholine neurons contributes to the memory loss of <b>Alzheimer's disease</b>, treated (modestly) with <b>cholinesterase inhibitors</b> that slow its breakdown. <b>Myasthenia gravis</b> attacks ACh receptors at the muscle, causing weakness. Toxins act here dramatically: <b>botulinum</b> blocks ACh release (paralysis; also "Botox"), <b>curare</b> blocks its receptors, and <b>nerve agents</b> block its breakdown, causing fatal over-stimulation.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">5.8</span>The neuromodulators and unconventional messengers</h2>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Messenger</th><th>Role</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Endorphins / enkephalins</td><td>Natural opioids, dampen pain, produce pleasure; opioid drugs mimic them</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Histamine</td><td>Promotes wakefulness, which is why antihistamines cause drowsiness</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Endocannabinoids</td><td>Retrograde messengers that dial signalling down; the target of THC</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Nitric oxide</td><td>A gas that diffuses freely as a fast, local retrograde signal</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">5.9</span>How drugs act: agonists and antagonists</h2>
 <p>Every drug that touches the brain works somewhere in the synaptic cycle. <b>Agonists</b> boost a transmitter's effect, by mimicking it, increasing its release, or blocking its reuptake or breakdown. <b>Antagonists</b> reduce it, by blocking receptors or preventing release. Mapping a drug onto this cycle predicts both its therapeutic effect and its side effects.</p>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Case to reason about</div><p>A patient reports low mood, poor sleep and loss of appetite, with normal movement and memory. A drug that blocks the <b>serotonin</b> reuptake transporter is prescribed. Which transmitter system is being targeted, and by which mechanism? <b>Serotonin, via reuptake inhibition (an SSRI)</b>, leaving more serotonin active in the synapse.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Whether a neurotransmitter excites or inhibits a neuron depends mainly on…", "opts": ["Its colour", "The receptor it binds", "The axon length", "The myelin"], "a": 1, "why": "The same transmitter can excite or inhibit depending on the receptor type it acts on."}, {"q": "Benzodiazepines, barbiturates and alcohol all sedate by enhancing…", "opts": ["Glutamate", "Dopamine", "GABA", "Acetylcholine"], "a": 2, "why": "They potentiate GABA-A receptors, the brain's main inhibitory 'brake'."}, {"q": "Dopamine is best described as signalling…", "opts": ["Pure pleasure", "Reward prediction error", "Pain", "Sleep"], "a": 1, "why": "Dopamine fires to better-than-expected reward, a prediction-error signal that drives learning."}, {"q": "SSRIs treat depression primarily by…", "opts": ["Adding dopamine", "Blocking serotonin reuptake so it lingers", "Destroying receptors", "Boosting GABA"], "a": 1, "why": "SSRIs block the serotonin reuptake transporter, leaving more active in the synapse."}, {"q": "Loss of which transmitter's neurons contributes most to Alzheimer's memory loss?", "opts": ["Acetylcholine", "GABA", "Norepinephrine", "Glutamate"], "a": 0, "why": "Cholinergic (acetylcholine) neuron loss is central to Alzheimer's, hence cholinesterase-inhibitor treatment."}, {"q": "A drug that blocks a receptor to reduce a transmitter's effect is a(n)…", "opts": ["Agonist", "Antagonist", "Reuptake enhancer", "Vesicle"], "a": 1, "why": "Antagonists reduce signalling, often by blocking receptors; agonists boost it."}]},
{id:5,c:"var(--violet)",num:"Lesson 06",title:"Brain Development",sub:"From a folding neural tube to a refined adult brain, and why timing shapes everything.",lead:"Your brain wasn't built all at once. It assembled itself across decades on a schedule that explains a great deal about how we change as we grow, and why the timing of experience matters so much.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">6.1</span>The timeline of a brain</h2>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 6.1 · Brain development across the lifespan</div>${DIAG.devTimeline}<div class="figcap">From the prenatal neural tube, through an explosion of synapses in infancy, myelination and refinement in childhood, large-scale pruning and prefrontal maturation in adolescence, to ongoing specialisation in adulthood.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">6.2</span>Building the brain before birth</h2>
 <p>Around three weeks after conception, a sheet of cells folds into the <b>neural tube</b>, the origin of the entire nervous system. From it, neurons are produced at an astonishing rate (up to 250,000 per minute at peak), then <b>migrate</b> to their destinations (often guided by glia), <b>differentiate</b> into specific types, and begin wiring up. This sequence, proliferation, migration, differentiation, must run on schedule; disruptions underlie many developmental disorders.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance · teratogens</div><p>The prenatal brain is highly vulnerable to <b>teratogens</b>, alcohol, certain infections, drugs, and toxins. Prenatal alcohol exposure can cause <b>fetal alcohol spectrum disorder</b>, with lasting cognitive and behavioural effects, because alcohol disrupts neuron migration and survival at critical moments.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">6.3</span>Build big, then prune</h2>
 <p>A surprising principle governs development: the brain deliberately <b>overproduces</b> connections early, then <b>prunes</b> the ones it doesn't use. Synaptic density peaks in the first few years, far higher than in adulthood, and is then sculpted down by experience. Microglia carry out much of the pruning.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 6.2 · Synaptic density over a lifetime</div>${DIAG.synapticDensity}<div class="figcap">Synapse numbers rise steeply after birth and peak in early childhood, then are pruned through childhood and adolescence. 'Use it or lose it': exercised connections survive; the rest are cleared.</div></div>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · two flavours of plasticity</div><p>Development relies on <b>experience-expectant</b> plasticity, the brain "expects" universal inputs like light and language and wires itself around them during set windows, and <b>experience-dependent</b> plasticity, which tailors wiring to each individual's unique experiences throughout life. Myelination, meanwhile, continues for decades, speeding circuits from the back of the brain forward.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">6.4</span>Critical and sensitive periods</h2>
 <p>Some abilities have <b>windows</b> when the brain is especially primed to wire them. In their Nobel-winning work, <b>Hubel and Wiesel</b> showed that depriving one eye of input early in life permanently rewires visual cortex in favour of the open eye, the basis for treating childhood "lazy eye" (amblyopia) early. Language, too, is learned most easily before puberty; miss the window and the same learning becomes far harder, though rarely impossible.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">6.5</span>The teenage brain</h2>
 <div class="callout key"><div class="ct">🔑 Accelerator before brakes</div><p>In adolescence the emotional and reward systems (the limbic system, with surging dopamine) mature <b>before</b> the prefrontal "brakes" finish developing around age 25. The accelerator comes online before the brake, a neurological reason teens feel emotions intensely, seek novelty, and take more risks, especially among peers. It is a construction schedule, not a character flaw.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">6.6</span>The aging brain, and nature meets nurture</h2>
 <p>Healthy aging brings gradual changes, some shrinkage, slower processing, but also preserved or improved abilities like vocabulary and accumulated knowledge. Lifelong learning, exercise and social engagement build <b>cognitive reserve</b> that buffers against decline. Throughout, development reflects an interplay of genes and environment, increasingly understood through <b>epigenetics</b>: experience can switch genes on or off without changing the DNA itself, and some of these marks can even persist.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Because the prefrontal cortex isn't finished until the mid-twenties, the same injury affects a child, a teenager and an adult differently. Younger brains are often <i>more</i> able to reorganise around damage thanks to greater plasticity, but they are also more vulnerable during the sensitive windows when key circuits are being laid down.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "The entire nervous system develops from which embryonic structure?", "opts": ["The cerebellum", "The neural tube", "The corpus callosum", "The brainstem"], "a": 1, "why": "The neural tube forms early in gestation and gives rise to the whole nervous system."}, {"q": "'Synaptic pruning' refers to…", "opts": ["Growing new neurons", "Removing unused connections", "Producing dopamine", "Thickening myelin"], "a": 1, "why": "The brain overproduces synapses, then prunes the unused ones, 'use it or lose it'."}, {"q": "Hubel and Wiesel's deprivation experiments demonstrated…", "opts": ["Adult neurogenesis", "A critical period for visual development", "The dopamine reward system", "Reflex arcs"], "a": 1, "why": "Depriving an eye early permanently altered visual cortex, revealing a critical period."}, {"q": "Why do teenagers tend toward risk-taking, neurologically speaking?", "opts": ["Their amygdala is missing", "Reward/limbic systems mature before the prefrontal 'brakes'", "They have fewer neurons", "Their cerebellum is overactive"], "a": 1, "why": "The emotional/reward systems come online before the prefrontal control system finishes maturing."}, {"q": "Epigenetics describes how…", "opts": ["DNA sequence is rewritten by experience", "Experience switches genes on or off without changing the DNA", "Neurons fire", "Myelin forms"], "a": 1, "why": "Epigenetic marks alter gene expression in response to experience without changing the DNA sequence itself."}]},
{id:6,c:"var(--coral)",num:"Lesson 07",title:"Neuroplasticity",sub:"The brain is not fixed hardware, it rewires itself with every experience. The mechanisms, the limits, and how to harness it.",lead:"For most of the 20th century, scientists believed the adult brain was fixed. They were wrong. The brain physically reshapes its structure and function throughout life in response to experience, learning and injury. This is neuroplasticity, the biological basis of all learning.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">7.1</span>Two kinds of plasticity</h2>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 7.1 · Structural vs functional plasticity</div>${DIAG.plasticityTypes}<div class="figcap">Structural plasticity is physical change, new dendritic spines, axonal sprouting, even new neurons. Functional plasticity is the brain reassigning a job from a damaged region to a healthy one, as in stroke recovery.</div></div>
 <div class="termgrid">
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Structural plasticity</div><div class="td">Physical change: new synapses and dendritic spines, axonal sprouting, thicker myelin, and neurogenesis in the hippocampus.</div></div>
 <div class="term"><div class="tn">Functional plasticity</div><div class="td">Reassigning a function from a damaged area to an intact one, the basis of much recovery.</div></div>
 </div>

 <h2><span class="num">7.2</span>The cellular rule: Hebb, LTP and LTD</h2>
 <div class="callout key"><div class="ct">🔑 Fire together, wire together</div><p>Donald Hebb's principle: when two neurons repeatedly activate together, the connection between them strengthens. The cellular mechanism is <b>long-term potentiation (LTP)</b>, and its counterpart, <b>long-term depression (LTD)</b>, weakens connections that aren't used. This bidirectional tuning is learning, written at the synapse.</p></div>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 7.2 · Long-term potentiation (LTP)</div>${DIAG.ltp}<div class="figcap">A weak connection is strengthened by repeated co-activation, more receptors, a stronger synapse, an easier path for the next signal. Practice literally rebuilds the circuit.</div></div>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · the molecular machinery</div><p>LTP hinges on the glutamate <b>NMDA receptor</b>, a "coincidence detector": it only opens fully when the sending neuron releases glutamate <i>and</i> the receiving neuron is already depolarised. The resulting <b>calcium</b> influx triggers more <b>AMPA receptors</b> to be inserted into the membrane, making the synapse permanently more responsive, and, over time, structural growth of new dendritic spines.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">7.3</span>Plasticity you can see</h2>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Classic case · London taxi drivers</div><p>Drivers who spend years memorising London's streets to pass "The Knowledge" grow a measurably larger posterior <b>hippocampus</b>, the navigation centre. The longer they've driven, the larger the change, and it partly reverses after retirement. Hard proof that sustained experience physically reshapes the adult brain.</p></div>
 <ul>
 <li><b>Cortical remapping:</b> after amputation, the cortex for the missing limb is invaded by neighbours, the basis of <b>phantom limb</b> sensations, which Ramachandran famously eased with mirror therapy.</li>
 <li><b>Sensory substitution:</b> in people who are blind, the visual cortex is recruited to process touch (Braille) and hearing.</li>
 <li><b>Expertise:</b> musicians show enlarged motor and auditory regions tuned to their instrument.</li>
 </ul>

 <h2><span class="num">7.4</span>Recovery from injury, guided plasticity</h2>
 <p>Rehabilitation after stroke or brain injury is essentially <b>guided neuroplasticity</b>: repeated, structured practice that coaxes healthy tissue to take over lost jobs. <b>Constraint-induced movement therapy</b>, which forces use of an affected limb by restraining the good one, drives exactly this functional reorganisation.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">7.5</span>How to harness it</h2>
 <p>Plasticity favours activity that is <b>effortful, repeated and rewarded</b>. <b>Sleep</b> consolidates the day's changes; <b>aerobic exercise</b> raises growth factors like <b>BDNF</b> that support new connections; and <b>spaced practice</b> beats cramming because each retrieval re-strengthens the trace. The 7-Day Rewire Challenge in your missions is built directly on these principles.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance · the dark side</div><p>Plasticity is morally neutral, it strengthens whatever is practised. <b>Maladaptive plasticity</b> underlies chronic pain (pain circuits that sensitise and persist), <b>addiction</b> (reward circuits rewired around a drug), and <b>tinnitus</b> (auditory cortex remapping after hearing loss). The same mechanism that builds skill can also entrench harm, which is why how we repeatedly use our brains genuinely matters.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Neuroplasticity is best defined as the brain's ability to…", "opts": ["Stay fixed after childhood", "Change structure and function through experience", "Produce neurotransmitters", "Control the heartbeat"], "a": 1, "why": "Plasticity is the brain reshaping itself in response to experience, learning and injury."}, {"q": "The NMDA receptor is described as a 'coincidence detector' because it opens only when…", "opts": ["The cell is at rest", "Glutamate is released and the cell is already depolarised", "GABA binds", "Dopamine is present"], "a": 1, "why": "NMDA needs both presynaptic glutamate and postsynaptic depolarisation, detecting co-activation, the basis of LTP."}, {"q": "London taxi drivers famously show enlargement of which structure?", "opts": ["Amygdala", "Cerebellum", "Hippocampus", "Thalamus"], "a": 2, "why": "Years of spatial navigation enlarge the posterior hippocampus, the brain's mapping centre."}, {"q": "Constraint-induced movement therapy promotes recovery by…", "opts": ["Resting the affected limb", "Forcing use of the affected limb to drive reorganisation", "Blocking plasticity", "Removing the good limb"], "a": 1, "why": "Forcing use of the affected limb harnesses functional plasticity to rebuild lost function."}, {"q": "Chronic pain, addiction and tinnitus are examples of…", "opts": ["Synaptic pruning", "Maladaptive plasticity", "Reflex arcs", "Homeostasis"], "a": 1, "why": "They are maladaptive plasticity, the brain strengthening unhelpful circuits through repetition."}]},
{id:7,c:"var(--teal)",num:"Lesson 08",title:"Memory & Learning",sub:"How the brain encodes, stores and retrieves experience, and the many ways memory can fail.",lead:"Memory is not a single thing or a single place. It is a set of systems that capture, hold and reconstruct information, each with its own biology. Understanding them explains everything from why cramming fails to why a smell can summon a childhood afternoon.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">8.1</span>Three stages: the modal model</h2>
 <p>The classic Atkinson–Shiffrin model describes information flowing through three stores. <b>Sensory memory</b> holds a near-complete snapshot of the senses for a fraction of a second. Whatever you <b>attend to</b> passes into <b>short-term / working memory</b>, which holds only about <b>7 items for a handful of seconds</b>. With <b>rehearsal and encoding</b>, some of it is consolidated into <b>long-term memory</b>, whose capacity is effectively limitless and whose contents can last a lifetime.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 8.1 · The memory systems</div>${DIAG.memorySystems}<div class="figcap">Information flows from a brief sensory buffer, through a limited working store, into long-term memory, which divides into declarative ('knowing that') and procedural ('knowing how'). Most information decays before it is ever consolidated.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">8.2</span>Working memory, the mental workspace</h2>
 <p>Alan Baddeley refined "short-term memory" into <b>working memory</b>: an active workspace, not a passive store. It includes a <b>phonological loop</b> (verbal/auditory rehearsal, your inner voice), a <b>visuospatial sketchpad</b> (mental imagery), an <b>episodic buffer</b> that binds them, and a <b>central executive</b> that directs attention. Working memory sits largely in the <b>prefrontal cortex</b>, and its limited capacity is the bottleneck behind much of cognition. We expand it not by adding slots but by <b>chunking</b>, grouping items into meaningful units.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">8.3</span>The many kinds of long-term memory</h2>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Type</th><th>Subtype</th><th>Example</th></tr>
 <tr><td rowspan="2">Declarative (explicit)</td><td>Episodic</td><td>Your last birthday, events you can recollect</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Semantic</td><td>Paris is the capital of France, facts</td></tr>
 <tr><td rowspan="2">Non-declarative (implicit)</td><td>Procedural</td><td>Riding a bike, skills and habits</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Conditioning / priming</td><td>Flinching at a sound; faster recognition after exposure</td></tr></table>
 <p>These rely on different brain systems: declarative memory depends on the <b>hippocampus and medial temporal lobe</b>, procedural memory on the <b>basal ganglia and cerebellum</b>, and emotional memory on the <b>amygdala</b>. This is why a patient can lose one and keep the others.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">8.4</span>Encoding, consolidation and retrieval</h2>
 <p>A memory is built in three steps: <b>encoding</b> (getting it in, deeper, meaningful processing beats rote repetition), <b>consolidation</b> (stabilising it, heavily dependent on <b>sleep</b> and on the synaptic strengthening of LTP from Lesson 7), and <b>retrieval</b> (getting it back out, which is itself a reconstructive act). Retrieval is also a powerful learning event, the <b>testing effect</b> means recalling something strengthens it more than re-reading it, and <b>spacing</b> practice beats cramming.</p>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · memory is reconstructive</div><p>We don't replay memories like a video; we rebuild them each time, filling gaps with expectation. Every retrieval can subtly rewrite the memory (<b>reconsolidation</b>), which is why confident memories can still be wrong.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">8.5</span>Forgetting and false memory</h2>
 <p>Forgetting isn't only decay; it includes <b>interference</b> (old and new memories competing) and <b>retrieval failure</b> (the trace exists but the cue is missing, the tip-of-the-tongue state). Memory is also <b>suggestible</b>: Elizabeth Loftus showed that leading questions and misinformation can implant detailed memories of events that never happened, findings with profound implications for eyewitness testimony.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">8.6</span>When memory breaks</h2>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p><b>Anterograde amnesia</b> (cannot form new memories, as in H.M.) is dissociable from <b>retrograde amnesia</b> (loss of memories formed before an injury). <b>Korsakoff's syndrome</b>, from thiamine deficiency in chronic alcohol use, devastates memory and prompts <b>confabulation</b>, filling gaps with invented detail. <b>Alzheimer's disease</b> attacks the hippocampus first, which is why recent episodic memory fades earliest while old skills and distant memories linger.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Working memory holds roughly…", "opts": ["Unlimited items for years", "About 7 items for seconds", "One item for hours", "Nothing measurable"], "a": 1, "why": "Short-term/working memory is famously limited to about seven items for a few seconds."}, {"q": "Riding a bike is an example of which memory type?", "opts": ["Episodic", "Semantic", "Procedural", "Sensory"], "a": 2, "why": "Skills and habits are procedural (implicit) memory, relying on the basal ganglia and cerebellum."}, {"q": "Which process most strengthens a memory according to research?", "opts": ["Re-reading repeatedly", "Actively retrieving it (testing)", "Highlighting", "Cramming the night before"], "a": 1, "why": "The testing effect: retrieval practice strengthens memory more than passive review."}, {"q": "Elizabeth Loftus's work showed that memory is…", "opts": ["A perfect recording", "Suggestible and reconstructive", "Stored only in the amygdala", "Impossible to change"], "a": 1, "why": "Misinformation can implant false memories, memory is reconstructive, not a recording."}, {"q": "Being unable to form NEW memories after an injury is called…", "opts": ["Retrograde amnesia", "Anterograde amnesia", "Confabulation", "Priming"], "a": 1, "why": "Anterograde amnesia is the loss of the ability to form new memories going forward."}]},
{id:8,c:"var(--coral)",num:"Lesson 09",title:"Emotion & Motivation",sub:"What drives behaviour, the theories of emotion, the brain's reward system, and how motivation is hijacked.",lead:"Emotions are not the opposite of reason; they are rapid appraisals that guide decisions, focus attention and prepare the body to act. Motivation is the engine that turns those signals into behaviour. Both run on circuits we can now map.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">9.1</span>What is an emotion?</h2>
 <p>An emotion bundles three components: a <b>physiological</b> response (heart, hormones, muscles), an <b>expressive</b> response (face, posture, voice), and a <b>subjective</b> feeling. A long-running debate asks how these relate, and which comes first.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">9.2</span>Theories of emotion</h2>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 9.1 · Three classic theories of emotion</div>${DIAG.emotionTheories}<div class="figcap">James–Lange: the body reacts first and we read the feeling off it. Cannon–Bard: body and feeling arise together. Schachter–Singer: arousal plus a cognitive label produces the specific emotion (the two-factor theory).</div></div>
 <ul>
 <li><b>James–Lange:</b> we feel afraid <i>because</i> our heart is already pounding, emotion is the perception of bodily change.</li>
 <li><b>Cannon–Bard:</b> the bodily response and the conscious feeling happen <b>simultaneously and independently</b>.</li>
 <li><b>Schachter–Singer (two-factor):</b> arousal is general; we <b>label</b> it based on context to produce a specific emotion.</li>
 <li><b>Appraisal theory (Lazarus):</b> how we <b>interpret</b> a situation determines the emotion, the same event can anger or amuse depending on appraisal.</li>
 </ul>

 <h2><span class="num">9.3</span>The emotional brain</h2>
 <p>Emotion runs largely on the <b>limbic system</b> (Lesson 3). The <b>amygdala</b> detects threat and tags memories with feeling; the <b>hypothalamus</b> drives the bodily response; the <b>prefrontal cortex</b> appraises and <b>regulates</b>, dampening the amygdala when we reframe a situation. Emotion regulation, the ability to reinterpret or soothe, is essentially the prefrontal cortex modulating the limbic system.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">9.4</span>Motivation and the reward system</h2>
 <p>Motivation is driven heavily by the <b>mesolimbic dopamine system</b>, the ventral tegmental area projecting to the nucleus accumbens. As we saw, dopamine signals <b>reward prediction error</b> and, crucially, fuels <b>"wanting"</b> (the drive to pursue) more than "liking" (the pleasure itself). Psychologists distinguish <b>intrinsic motivation</b> (doing something for its own satisfaction) from <b>extrinsic motivation</b> (for an external reward), and find that excessive external rewards can sometimes erode intrinsic drive.</p>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · drives and homeostasis</div><p>Many motivations are <b>homeostatic</b>, hunger, thirst and temperature regulation pull the body back to a set point, orchestrated by the hypothalamus. Others, like curiosity and social connection, are not about restoring a deficit at all, which is why simple "drive-reduction" theories are incomplete.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">9.5</span>When motivation is hijacked, addiction</h2>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Addictive drugs flood the mesolimbic system with dopamine far beyond any natural reward, teaching the brain to crave the drug above all else. Over time the system adapts: tolerance grows, natural rewards feel flat, and the prefrontal "brakes" weaken, a vicious cycle of <b>maladaptive plasticity</b>. This is why addiction is understood as a brain disorder of learning and motivation, not merely a failure of willpower.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">9.6</span>Stress, emotion and health</h2>
 <p>Acute stress (the sympathetic surge and cortisol of Lesson 1) sharpens focus and mobilises energy. But chronic stress keeps the system switched on, contributing to anxiety, depression, impaired memory (via hippocampal damage), and physical illness. Emotion and physiology are inseparable, which is why managing stress is a genuinely biological intervention.</p>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "The James–Lange theory proposes that…", "opts": ["Feeling causes bodily change", "Bodily change comes first and we read emotion from it", "Emotion and arousal are unrelated", "Only the amygdala matters"], "a": 1, "why": "James–Lange: we feel afraid because we notice our body is already reacting."}, {"q": "Schachter–Singer's two-factor theory adds which ingredient to arousal?", "opts": ["A cognitive label/interpretation", "More adrenaline", "A reflex", "Sleep"], "a": 0, "why": "Two-factor theory says arousal plus a cognitive label yields the specific emotion."}, {"q": "The mesolimbic dopamine system is most associated with…", "opts": ["Balance", "Reward and motivation ('wanting')", "Breathing", "Vision"], "a": 1, "why": "Mesolimbic dopamine drives reward learning and the motivational pull to pursue rewards."}, {"q": "Emotion regulation largely involves the…", "opts": ["Cerebellum calming the cortex", "Prefrontal cortex modulating the amygdala", "Brainstem labelling feelings", "Hippocampus producing dopamine"], "a": 1, "why": "The prefrontal cortex appraises and dampens limbic (amygdala) responses."}, {"q": "Addiction is best described as…", "opts": ["A simple lack of willpower", "Maladaptive plasticity hijacking the reward system", "A purely social problem", "An allergy"], "a": 1, "why": "Addiction rewires the reward and motivation circuits, a brain disorder of learning."}]},
{id:9,c:"var(--violet)",num:"Lesson 10",title:"Attention, Consciousness & Sleep",sub:"The spotlight of the mind, the mystery of awareness, and the nightly cycle that keeps the brain healthy.",lead:"Your brain receives far more information than it can process. Attention selects what matters; consciousness is the awareness that results; and sleep is the nightly maintenance that makes all of it possible. Together they govern the moment-to-moment experience of being you.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">10.1</span>Attention, the brain's spotlight</h2>
 <p>Attention is the selective focusing of limited processing resources. <b>Selective attention</b> lets you follow one conversation in a noisy room, the "cocktail party effect", while filtering the rest (though your name still cuts through). <b>Divided attention</b> (multitasking) is mostly an illusion: the brain rapidly switches rather than truly parallel-processing, and each switch costs time and accuracy.</p>
 <div class="deepbox"><div class="dt">Going deeper · the Stroop effect</div><p>When the word "RED" is printed in blue ink, naming the ink colour is slow and error-prone because reading is automatic and <b>interferes</b> with the colour-naming task. This Stroop interference is a classic measure of attention and executive control, and you can try it yourself in the <b>Stroop Test</b> mission.</p></div>
 <p>Failures of attention are striking: in <b>inattentional blindness</b>, people focused on one task can miss an obvious unexpected event (the famous "invisible gorilla"). We see far less of the world than we believe.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">10.2</span>Consciousness</h2>
 <p>Consciousness, subjective awareness, remains one of science's deepest puzzles. A leading framework, <b>Global Workspace Theory</b>, proposes that information becomes conscious when it is "broadcast" widely across the brain, available to many systems at once, rather than processed in an isolated module. Much of what the brain does, though, is <b>unconscious</b>: the blindsight patients of Lesson 2 act on visual information they cannot consciously see.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">10.3</span>The architecture of sleep</h2>
 <p>Sleep is not a uniform "off" state. It cycles roughly every 90 minutes through stages, defined by brain-wave patterns: light sleep (N1, N2), deep slow-wave sleep (N3), and <b>REM</b> (rapid-eye-movement) sleep, when the brain is highly active and most vivid dreaming occurs while the body is paralysed.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 10.1 · A night of sleep (hypnogram)</div>${DIAG.sleepStages}<div class="figcap">Across the night the brain cycles between stages. Deep slow-wave sleep dominates early cycles; REM periods lengthen toward morning. The whole cycle repeats four to five times.</div></div>

 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure · The sleep cycle</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="The ~90-minute sleep cycle and its stages"><div class="figcap">A single ~90-minute cycle moves through light sleep, deep sleep and REM, repeating four to five times a night, with REM lengthening toward morning.</div></div><h2><span class="num">10.4</span>Why we sleep</h2>
 <p>Sleep is active maintenance, not idleness. Deep slow-wave sleep <b>consolidates memories</b>, replaying the day's hippocampal activity to the cortex. The <b>glymphatic system</b> clears metabolic waste, including the proteins implicated in Alzheimer's, far more efficiently during sleep. Sleep also restores energy, regulates emotion and supports immune function. Chronic sleep loss impairs attention, memory, mood and health, and is linked to a range of diseases.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">10.5</span>Dreaming and disorders</h2>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Sleep disorders include <b>insomnia</b>, <b>sleep apnea</b> (repeated breathing pauses that fragment sleep), and <b>narcolepsy</b> (sudden sleep attacks and intrusions of REM). Disorders of consciousness form a spectrum: <b>coma</b> (no arousal or awareness), the <b>vegetative state</b> (arousal without awareness), and <b>locked-in syndrome</b> (full awareness without the ability to move), distinctions that depend on the brainstem and cortex and carry profound ethical weight.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Following one conversation in a noisy room demonstrates…", "opts": ["Divided attention", "Selective attention", "Inattentional blindness", "REM sleep"], "a": 1, "why": "Selective attention focuses resources on one stream while filtering others, the cocktail party effect."}, {"q": "The Stroop effect shows that…", "opts": ["Colour naming is automatic", "Reading is automatic and interferes with naming ink colour", "People are colour-blind", "Attention is unlimited"], "a": 1, "why": "Automatic reading interferes with naming the ink colour, slowing responses, a measure of attentional control."}, {"q": "Vivid dreaming and bodily paralysis are hallmarks of which sleep stage?", "opts": ["N1", "N3 deep sleep", "REM", "Wakefulness"], "a": 2, "why": "REM sleep features an active, dreaming brain with the body's muscles paralysed."}, {"q": "Which function is strongly supported by deep slow-wave sleep?", "opts": ["Memory consolidation and waste clearance", "Vision", "Digestion", "Reflexes"], "a": 0, "why": "Deep sleep consolidates memory and the glymphatic system clears metabolic waste."}, {"q": "Arousal without any awareness describes the…", "opts": ["Locked-in syndrome", "Vegetative state", "Coma", "REM state"], "a": 1, "why": "The vegetative state involves wake–sleep cycles (arousal) but no signs of awareness."}]},
{id:10,c:"var(--amber)",num:"Lesson 11",title:"Language & the Lateralised Brain",sub:"How the brain produces and understands language, and what split-brain patients reveal about two minds in one skull.",lead:"Language is among the most lateralised of human abilities, leaning heavily on a network in the left hemisphere. Mapping that network, and studying what happens when it is damaged or disconnected, has been one of neuropsychology's great success stories.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">11.1</span>The language network</h2>
 <p>Two regions, usually in the left hemisphere, anchor language. <b>Broca's area</b> in the frontal lobe assembles fluent, grammatical <b>speech output</b>. <b>Wernicke's area</b> in the temporal lobe handles <b>comprehension</b>, attaching meaning to words. They are linked by a fibre bundle, the <b>arcuate fasciculus</b>, that lets comprehension and production work together.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 11.1 · The classic language network</div>${DIAG.languageNetwork}<div class="figcap">Broca's area (frontal) produces speech; Wernicke's area (temporal) comprehends it; the arcuate fasciculus connects them, near the auditory cortex. Damage to each part produces a distinct pattern of impairment.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">11.2</span>The aphasias</h2>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Aphasia</th><th>Damage</th><th>Speech</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Broca's (expressive)</td><td>Broca's area</td><td>Halting, effortful, telegraphic; comprehension largely intact; aware of the deficit</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Wernicke's (receptive)</td><td>Wernicke's area</td><td>Fluent but meaningless; poor comprehension; often unaware</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Conduction</td><td>Arcuate fasciculus</td><td>Good comprehension and fluency, but cannot repeat words</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Global</td><td>Both regions</td><td>Severe loss of both production and comprehension</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">11.3</span>Lateralisation, a divided brain</h2>
 <p>In most people the <b>left</b> hemisphere dominates language and sequential, detailed processing, while the <b>right</b> excels at spatial relationships, faces, prosody (the music of speech) and the global "gist." This is a matter of degree, not the cartoon of "left-brained" versus "right-brained" personalities. The two halves normally cooperate seamlessly through the <b>corpus callosum</b>.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">11.4</span>The split brain</h2>
 <div class="callout case"><div class="ct">🧪 Classic case · Sperry &amp; Gazzaniga</div><p>When the corpus callosum is cut to control severe epilepsy, the hemispheres can be tested separately. Flash an image to the <b>right hemisphere</b> only (left visual field) and the patient cannot <i>name</i> it, language lives in the disconnected left hemisphere, yet the left hand can pick the object out by touch. Sometimes the verbal left hemisphere even <b>confabulates</b> a reason for an action the right hemisphere initiated, revealing how the narrating mind constructs explanations after the fact. The work won a Nobel Prize.</p></div>

 <h2><span class="num">11.5</span>Acquiring language</h2>
 <p>Children acquire language with astonishing ease, suggesting a strong biological readiness (Chomsky's idea of an innate capacity), shaped by rich input. There appears to be a <b>sensitive period</b>: children deprived of language early, or learning a second language after puberty, rarely reach full native mastery, echoing the critical-period principle of Lesson 6.</p>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p><b>Developmental dyslexia</b>, difficulty with reading despite normal intelligence and instruction, is linked to differences in the brain's language and visual-word regions, not to laziness or low ability. Recognising its neural basis transformed how it is supported.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Broca's aphasia is characterised by…", "opts": ["Fluent, meaningless speech", "Halting, effortful speech with intact comprehension", "Total blindness", "No symptoms"], "a": 1, "why": "Broca's (expressive) aphasia produces effortful, telegraphic speech with comprehension largely preserved."}, {"q": "Wernicke's area is mainly responsible for…", "opts": ["Producing speech", "Comprehending language", "Vision", "Balance"], "a": 1, "why": "Wernicke's area handles language comprehension; damage yields fluent but meaningless speech."}, {"q": "The arcuate fasciculus connects…", "opts": ["The two hemispheres", "Broca's and Wernicke's areas", "Eye and thalamus", "Cerebellum and cortex"], "a": 1, "why": "The arcuate fasciculus links the production and comprehension hubs; its damage causes conduction aphasia."}, {"q": "In split-brain patients, an image shown only to the right hemisphere cannot be named because…", "opts": ["The right hemisphere is blind", "Language is usually left-lateralised and disconnected", "The image is too dim", "The thalamus is cut"], "a": 1, "why": "Language sits in the left hemisphere, which is disconnected from the right after callosal section."}, {"q": "The fact that late second-language learners rarely reach native mastery reflects a…", "opts": ["Lack of effort", "Sensitive period for language", "Memory disorder", "Hearing problem"], "a": 1, "why": "Language learning has a sensitive period, easiest before puberty."}]},
{id:11,c:"var(--teal)",num:"Lesson 12",title:"How We Study the Brain",sub:"The methods of neuroscience and neuropsychology, from lesion studies to brain imaging and stimulation.",lead:"Every fact in this module came from a method. Each technique trades off precision in space, precision in time, and how invasive it is. Knowing the tools, and their limits, is what separates understanding the brain from merely memorising claims about it.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">12.1</span>The lesion method, learning from damage</h2>
 <p>The oldest approach in neuropsychology asks: when a specific region is damaged, what ability is lost? Cases like <b>Phineas Gage</b> (frontal lobe, personality) and <b>Patient H.M.</b> (hippocampus, memory) mapped function onto structure long before imaging existed. In animals, controlled lesions test causal claims directly. The limitation: natural damage is rarely neat, and losing a function tells you a region is <i>necessary</i>, not that it acts alone.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">12.2</span>Structural imaging, picturing anatomy</h2>
 <p><b>CT</b> (computed tomography) uses X-rays for a quick structural scan, good for bleeds and fractures. <b>MRI</b> (magnetic resonance imaging) uses powerful magnets for far sharper soft-tissue detail, with no radiation. <b>DTI</b> (diffusion tensor imaging) traces the white-matter tracts, the brain's wiring, by following the movement of water along fibres.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">12.3</span>Functional imaging, watching the brain work</h2>
 <p><b>fMRI</b> infers activity from changes in blood oxygenation (the BOLD signal): excellent spatial detail (millimetres) but sluggish timing (seconds). <b>PET</b> uses radioactive tracers to map metabolism or specific neurotransmitter systems. Both reveal <i>where</i> activity rises during a task, but correlation with a task is not proof that the region causes the behaviour.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 12.1 · Trade-offs between methods</div>${DIAG.imagingMethods}<div class="figcap">Each method balances spatial detail against temporal (timing) detail, and against how invasive it is. fMRI sees fine spatial structure slowly; EEG sees millisecond timing coarsely; single-unit recording is exquisitely precise but invasive.</div></div>

 <h2><span class="num">12.4</span>Electrophysiology, measuring electrical activity</h2>
 <p><b>EEG</b> records electrical activity from scalp electrodes with millisecond timing but poor spatial resolution; averaging EEG to repeated events yields <b>ERPs</b> (event-related potentials). <b>MEG</b> measures the tiny magnetic fields of neural activity with better localisation. <b>Single-unit recording</b> listens to one neuron at a time, how place cells were discovered, but is invasive, used mainly in animals or during neurosurgery.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">12.5</span>Stimulation, testing causation</h2>
 <p>Where imaging shows correlation, stimulation can test <b>causation</b>. <b>TMS</b> (transcranial magnetic stimulation) uses magnetic pulses to briefly excite or disrupt a cortical region from outside the skull, creating a temporary, reversible "virtual lesion." <b>tDCS</b> applies weak electrical current to nudge excitability. <b>DBS</b> (deep brain stimulation) implants electrodes to treat Parkinson's and other conditions by modulating deep circuits directly.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">12.6</span>The neuropsychologist's caution</h2>
 <div class="callout key"><div class="ct">🔑 Correlation is not causation</div><p>A region "lighting up" during a task only shows association. Strong claims require <b>converging evidence</b>, lesion data, stimulation, imaging and behaviour pointing the same way. Clinical <b>neuropsychological assessment</b> combines standardised cognitive tests with this evidence to localise problems and guide rehabilitation. Good science holds the limits of each method firmly in view.</p></div>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "The lesion method establishes that a damaged region is…", "opts": ["Sufficient for a function", "Necessary for a function", "Unrelated to behaviour", "Only decorative"], "a": 1, "why": "Losing a function after damage shows the region is necessary, not that it acts alone or is sufficient."}, {"q": "fMRI offers…", "opts": ["Millisecond timing, poor spatial detail", "Fine spatial detail but slow timing", "No spatial detail", "Direct single-neuron recording"], "a": 1, "why": "fMRI has good spatial resolution (mm) but slow temporal resolution (seconds)."}, {"q": "Which method has the best temporal (timing) resolution?", "opts": ["fMRI", "PET", "EEG", "CT"], "a": 2, "why": "EEG captures millisecond-scale electrical timing, though with poor spatial localisation."}, {"q": "TMS is valuable because it can…", "opts": ["Only image anatomy", "Temporarily disrupt a region to test causation", "Record one neuron", "Measure blood flow"], "a": 1, "why": "TMS creates a reversible 'virtual lesion', allowing causal tests of a region's role."}, {"q": "A brain region 'lighting up' during a task proves…", "opts": ["It causes the behaviour", "It is associated with the task", "It is damaged", "Nothing at all"], "a": 1, "why": "Imaging shows correlation/association; causal claims need converging methods like stimulation or lesions."}]},
{id:12,c:"var(--coral)",num:"Lesson 13",title:"Brain Disorders & Mental Health",sub:"When the brain is injured or dysregulated, neurological and psychiatric conditions, and the model that unites them.",lead:"Everything in this module matters most when something goes wrong. Brain disorders span the neurological and the psychiatric, but the old wall between 'brain diseases' and 'mind diseases' is dissolving, both are conditions of the same organ, best understood through multiple lenses at once.",html:`
 <h2><span class="num">13.1</span>Two traditions, one organ</h2>
 <p>Historically, <b>neurological</b> disorders (stroke, epilepsy, Parkinson's) were seen as diseases of the brain, and <b>psychiatric</b> disorders (depression, schizophrenia) as diseases of the mind. Modern neuroscience treats both as conditions of the nervous system, differing in how visible the physical changes are, not in whether the brain is involved.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">13.2</span>Neurodegenerative diseases</h2>
 <table class="dtable"><tr><th>Disease</th><th>Core pathology</th><th>Hallmark</th></tr>
 <tr><td>Alzheimer's</td><td>Amyloid plaques &amp; tau tangles; hippocampus hit first</td><td>Progressive memory loss</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Parkinson's</td><td>Loss of dopamine neurons (substantia nigra)</td><td>Tremor, rigidity, slowed movement</td></tr>
 <tr><td>Huntington's</td><td>Genetic; degeneration of the striatum</td><td>Involuntary movements, cognitive decline</td></tr></table>

 <h2><span class="num">13.3</span>Vascular and traumatic injury</h2>
 <p>A <b>stroke</b> cuts blood supply to part of the brain (by clot or bleed); the lost function depends entirely on the region affected, which is why localisation matters. <b>Traumatic brain injury (TBI)</b> ranges from concussion to severe damage, often hitting the frontal and temporal lobes. <b>Epilepsy</b> is a disorder of runaway synchronous firing, the imbalance of glutamate excitation and GABA inhibition from Lesson 5, producing seizures.</p>

 <h2><span class="num">13.4</span>Psychiatric conditions</h2>
 <ul>
 <li><b>Depression</b>, persistent low mood and loss of pleasure; linked to monoamine signalling, stress/HPA dysregulation, and reduced hippocampal neurogenesis.</li>
 <li><b>Anxiety disorders</b>, an overactive threat system (amygdala) and weakened prefrontal regulation.</li>
 <li><b>Schizophrenia</b>, disturbances of thought and perception (hallucinations, delusions); the dopamine hypothesis plus structural and connectivity changes.</li>
 <li><b>Bipolar disorder</b>, cycling between depression and mania, with a strong genetic component.</li>
 </ul>

 <h2><span class="num">13.5</span>The biopsychosocial model</h2>
 <p>No single lens explains mental health. The <b>biopsychosocial model</b> holds that biological factors (genes, neurochemistry), psychological factors (thoughts, coping, learning history) and social factors (relationships, environment, adversity) <b>interact</b> to shape both vulnerability and resilience.</p>
 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure 13.1 · The biopsychosocial model</div>${DIAG.biopsychosocial}<div class="figcap">Mental health emerges from the interaction of biological, psychological and social factors, no single level tells the whole story, and effective care usually addresses more than one.</div></div>

 <div class="fig"><div class="fig-label">Figure · Daily habits for a healthy brain</div><img class="fig-img" src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="Daily habits that protect brain health"><div class="figcap">Treatment is not only clinical. Everyday habits, good food, movement, learning, connection, sleep and stress management, protect the brain and support lifelong mental wellbeing.</div></div><h2><span class="num">13.6</span>Treatment, and stigma</h2>
 <div class="callout clin"><div class="ct">🩺 Clinical relevance</div><p>Treatments map onto these levels: <b>medications</b> adjust neurotransmission (SSRIs, antipsychotics, mood stabilisers); <b>psychotherapies</b> such as CBT retrain thought and behaviour, itself a form of guided neuroplasticity; <b>stimulation</b> (TMS, DBS, ECT) targets circuits directly; and <b>social support</b> changes the environment. Understanding disorders as brain-based, multi-level conditions, not personal failings, is central to reducing the stigma that keeps people from seeking help.</p></div>
 <p class="figcap" style="border:none;margin-top:18px;">This lesson touches on mental-health conditions. If any of it feels personally relevant, talking with a doctor or a trusted professional is a good step, these are common, understood, and treatable conditions.</p>
 `,quiz:[{"q": "Parkinson's disease is caused primarily by the loss of…", "opts": ["Serotonin neurons", "Dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra", "Hippocampal neurons", "Myelin"], "a": 1, "why": "Parkinson's results from degeneration of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra."}, {"q": "The effect of a stroke depends mainly on…", "opts": ["The patient's age only", "Which brain region loses blood supply", "The time of day", "Eye colour"], "a": 1, "why": "Lost function is determined by the location of the affected region, localisation matters."}, {"q": "Epilepsy reflects an imbalance between which two signals?", "opts": ["Dopamine and serotonin", "Glutamate excitation and GABA inhibition", "Myelin and axons", "Sleep and wakefulness"], "a": 1, "why": "Seizures arise from runaway excitation (glutamate) relative to inhibition (GABA)."}, {"q": "The biopsychosocial model states that mental health is shaped by…", "opts": ["Biology alone", "Biological, psychological and social factors interacting", "Willpower", "Social factors only"], "a": 1, "why": "It integrates biological, psychological and social levels rather than privileging one."}, {"q": "Cognitive behavioural therapy can be understood neurologically as…", "opts": ["A purely chemical fix", "A form of guided neuroplasticity", "Brain surgery", "A type of imaging"], "a": 1, "why": "CBT retrains thought and behaviour, reshaping neural circuits, guided plasticity."}]}
];
function renderLessons(){
  document.getElementById('lessonGrid').innerHTML=LESSONS.map(l=>`
    <div class="lesson-card" data-done="${S.lessons[l.id]?1:0}" style="--c:${l.c}" onclick="go('lesson',${l.id})">
      <div class="lc-num">${l.num}</div>
      <div class="lc-title">${l.title}</div>
      <div class="lc-sub">${l.sub}</div>
      <div class="lc-foot">
        <span class="lc-xp">+40 XP</span>
        <span class="lc-status">${S.lessons[l.id]?'✓ Complete':'Start →'}</span>
      </div>
    </div>`).join('');
}

function renderLesson(id){
  const l=LESSONS[id];if(!l)return;
  document.getElementById('readerProgress').textContent=l.num+' of '+String(LESSONS.length).padStart(2,'0');
  const done=!!S.lessons[id];
  document.getElementById('readerBody').innerHTML=`
    <div class="reader-eyebrow eyebrow">${l.num} · Core study notes</div>
    <h1>${l.title}</h1>
    <p class="reader-lead">${l.lead}</p>
    ${l.html}
    <div id="quizBox"></div>`;
  renderQuiz(l);
}

/* ── QUIZ ENGINE ── */
function renderQuiz(l){
  const box=document.getElementById('quizBox');
  const prev=S.quizzes[l.id];
  box.innerHTML=`
    <h2 style="margin-top:46px;">Check your understanding</h2>
    <p style="color:var(--ink-soft);margin-bottom:8px;">${l.quiz.length} questions · +20 XP each correct${prev?` · last score ${prev.correct}/${prev.total}`:''}</p>
    <div id="qList"></div>
    <div class="reader-foot">
      <button class="btn btn-primary" id="qSubmit">Submit quiz</button>
      <button class="btn btn-ghost" onclick="go('lessons')">Back to lessons</button>
    </div>`;
  const list=document.getElementById('qList');
  list.innerHTML=l.quiz.map((q,qi)=>`
    <div class="panel" style="margin:14px 0;">
      <div style="font-family:var(--disp);font-weight:700;font-size:16px;margin-bottom:14px;">${qi+1}. ${q.q}</div>
      <div class="qopts" data-qi="${qi}">
        ${q.opts.map((o,oi)=>`<button class="qopt" data-oi="${oi}" style="display:block;width:100%;text-align:left;background:var(--paper);border:1.5px solid var(--line);border-radius:11px;padding:12px 15px;margin-bottom:9px;font-size:14.5px;transition:.15s;">${o}</button>`).join('')}
      </div>
      <div class="qwhy" style="display:none;font-size:13.5px;border-radius:10px;padding:11px 14px;margin-top:4px;"></div>
    </div>`).join('');
  list.querySelectorAll('.qopts').forEach(grp=>{
    grp.querySelectorAll('.qopt').forEach(btn=>btn.addEventListener('click',()=>{
      grp.querySelectorAll('.qopt').forEach(b=>{b.style.borderColor='var(--line)';b.style.background='var(--paper)';b.classList.remove('sel');});
      btn.classList.add('sel');btn.style.borderColor='var(--violet)';btn.style.background='var(--violet-soft)';
    }));
  });
  document.getElementById('qSubmit').addEventListener('click',()=>gradeQuiz(l));
}
function gradeQuiz(l){
  let correct=0,answered=0;
  l.quiz.forEach((q,qi)=>{
    const grp=document.querySelector(`.qopts[data-qi="${qi}"]`);
    const sel=grp.querySelector('.qopt.sel');
    const why=grp.parentElement.querySelector('.qwhy');
    if(!sel)return; answered++;
    const oi=+sel.dataset.oi;
    grp.querySelectorAll('.qopt').forEach(b=>b.style.pointerEvents='none');
    const right=grp.querySelector(`.qopt[data-oi="${q.a}"]`);
    right.style.borderColor='var(--green)';right.style.background='var(--green-soft)';
    if(oi===q.a){correct++;why.style.background='var(--green-soft)';why.style.color='#3F7D5E';why.innerHTML='✓ '+q.why;}
    else{sel.style.borderColor='var(--coral)';sel.style.background='var(--coral-soft)';why.style.background='var(--coral-soft)';why.style.color='#B0614E';why.innerHTML='✗ '+q.why;}
    why.style.display='block';
  });
  if(answered<l.quiz.length){toast('🤔','Answer every question','A few are still blank','');return;}
  const firstTime=!S.quizzes[l.id];
  S.quizzes[l.id]={correct,total:l.quiz.length};
  let gained=correct*20;
  if(!S.lessons[l.id]){S.lessons[l.id]=Date.now();gained+=40;}
  S.xp+=gained;saveState();updateHUD();renderLessons();
  toast('⚡',`Quiz: ${correct}/${l.quiz.length} correct`,'Lesson marked complete','+'+gained+' XP');
  if(correct===l.quiz.length)unlockBadge('quizwhiz');
  if(LESSONS.every(x=>S.lessons[x.id]))unlockBadge('scholar');
  document.getElementById('qSubmit').textContent='Quiz complete ✓';
  document.getElementById('qSubmit').disabled=true;
}

/* ── PROGRESS / BADGES ── */
function renderBadgeShelf(){
  const shelf=document.getElementById('badgeShelf');if(!shelf)return;
  shelf.innerHTML=Object.entries(BADGES).map(([id,b])=>`
    <div class="bcell" data-earned="${S.badges[id]?1:0}">
      <div class="bi">${b.icon}</div>
      <div class="bn">${b.name}</div>
      <div class="bd">${b.desc}</div>
    </div>`).join('');
}
function renderProgress(){
  const r=rankFor(S.xp);
  document.getElementById('progRank').textContent=r.name;
  document.getElementById('progSub').textContent=`Level ${r.level} · ${r.isMax?'maximum rank reached':'keep firing those synapses'}`;
  document.getElementById('psXp').textContent=S.xp;
  document.getElementById('psLessons').textContent=LESSONS.filter(l=>S.lessons[l.id]).length+'/'+LESSONS.length;
  document.getElementById('psMissions').textContent=MISSIONS.filter(m=>S.missions[m.id]).length+'/'+MISSIONS.length;
  document.getElementById('psBadges').textContent=Object.keys(S.badges).length;
  renderBadgeShelf();
}
function resetProgress(){
  /* Native confirm() is blocked in some in-app/preview browsers, so use our own modal */
  const m=document.getElementById('resetConfirm');
  if(m){ m.classList.add('on'); }
  else if(confirm('Reset all XP, badges and progress? This cannot be undone.')){ doResetProgress(); }
}

async function doResetProgress(){
  const m=document.getElementById('resetConfirm');
  if(m)m.classList.remove('on');

  /* 1. Reset in-memory + local state */
  S=JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(DEFAULT_STATE));
  try{ localStorage.setItem('neuralquest_v1',JSON.stringify(S)); }catch(e){}

  /* 2. Clear the cloud copy too, if signed in */
  if(typeof sb!=='undefined' && sb && typeof currentUser!=='undefined' && currentUser){
    try{
      await sb.from('progress').upsert({user_id:currentUser.id,data:S,updated_at:new Date().toISOString()});
    }catch(e){ console.warn('Cloud reset failed:',e.message||e); }
  }

  /* 3. Re-render everything defensively — one failure shouldn't abort the rest */
  const safe=fn=>{ try{ fn(); }catch(e){ console.warn('render error:',e.message||e); } };
  safe(()=>boot());               // full re-render (HUD, lessons, missions, quests, badges, pathway)
  safe(()=>renderProgress());     // progress view, if built
  safe(()=>{ if(typeof renderProfile==='function') renderProfile(); });

  /* 4. Feedback + return home */
  safe(()=>toast('🔄','Progress reset','Fresh brain, fresh start',''));
  go('home');
}
</script>

<div id="toasts"></div>
<script>
/* ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
   MISSIONS
   ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ */
const MISSIONS=[
  {id:'build',icon:'🔬',name:'Build a Neuron',color:'var(--violet)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#ECE9FB,#FBE7DD)',
   desc:'Assemble a working neuron in the right order, then fire an action potential down its axon.'},
  {id:'explorer',icon:'🗺️',name:'Brain Districts',color:'var(--coral)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#FBE7DD,#FBEFD7)',
   desc:'Tour the 3D brain and open the dossier on five different regions to map the city inside your skull.'},
  {id:'mystery',icon:'🕵️',name:'Neurotransmitter Mystery',color:'var(--teal)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#E4EDF7,#E4F0E6)',
   desc:'Read the patient files and diagnose which chemical messenger is behind each case.'},
  {id:'rewire',icon:'🔁',name:'7-Day Rewire Challenge',color:'var(--green)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#E4F0E6,#ECE9FB)',
   desc:'Pick a micro-skill and train it for seven days. Watch neuroplasticity work in real time.'},
  {id:'label',icon:'🧩',name:'Label the Lobes',color:'var(--coral)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#FBE7DD,#ECE9FB)',
   desc:'Place each name on the right region of the brain and master the map of the cortex.'},
  {id:'match',icon:'🃏',name:'Memory Match',color:'var(--teal)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#E4EDF7,#E4F0E6)',
   desc:'Flip the cards and pair each brain structure with the job it does. A memory game about memory.'},
  {id:'stroop',icon:'🎨',name:'The Stroop Test',color:'var(--violet)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#ECE9FB,#E4EDF7)',
   desc:'Name the ink colour, not the word. Feel attention and interference for yourself in a real neuropsychology task.'},
  {id:'lesion',icon:'🧠',name:'Neuro-Detective',color:'var(--green)',bg:'linear-gradient(135deg,#E4F0E6,#FBEFD7)',
   desc:'Read the symptoms and localise the damage. Think like a clinical neuropsychologist.'}
];
function renderMissionGrid(){
  const html=MISSIONS.map(m=>`
    <div class="mission-card" data-done="${S.missions[m.id]?1:0}" onclick="openMission('${m.id}')">
      <div class="mc-top" style="background:${m.bg};color:${m.color}">${ICONS[MISSION_IC[m.id]]||m.icon}</div>
      <div class="mc-body">
        <div class="mc-title">${m.name}</div>
        <div class="mc-desc">${m.desc}</div>
        <div class="mc-foot"><span class="mc-go">${S.missions[m.id]?'Replay →':'Start mission →'}</span><span class="mc-done">● complete</span></div>
      </div>
    </div>`).join('');
  document.getElementById('missionGrid').innerHTML=html;
  const qg=document.getElementById('questMissionGrid');if(qg)qg.innerHTML=html;
}
function openMission(id){ if(id==='explorer'){go('explorer');return;} go('mission',id); }
function completeMission(id,badge,xp){
  const isNew=!S.missions[id];
  if(isNew){S.missions[id]=Date.now();S.xp+=xp;saveState();updateHUD();renderMissionGrid();renderQuests('questRow');}
  if(badge)unlockBadge(badge);
  if(['build','explorer','mystery','rewire'].every(m=>S.missions[m]))unlockBadge('questcrest');
  if(['label','match','stroop','lesion'].every(m=>S.missions[m]))unlockBadge('quest3crest');
  return isNew;
}
function buildMissionStage(id){
  if(id==='build')return missionBuild();
  if(id==='mystery')return missionMystery();
  if(id==='rewire')return missionRewire();
  if(id==='label')return missionLabel();
  if(id==='match')return missionMatch();
  if(id==='stroop')return missionStroop();
  if(id==='lesion')return missionLesion();
}

/* ── MISSION 1 · BUILD A NEURON ── */
const NEURON_PARTS=[
  {id:'dend',label:'Dendrites',icon:'🌿',hint:'Receive incoming signals'},
  {id:'soma',label:'Soma',icon:'⚪',hint:'Cell body sums the input'},
  {id:'axon',label:'Axon',icon:'➖',hint:'Carries the signal outward'},
  {id:'myelin',label:'Myelin Sheath',icon:'🟡',hint:'Insulates and speeds it up'},
  {id:'synapse',label:'Synapse',icon:'✨',hint:'Passes the message on'}
];
const NEURON_ORDER=['dend','soma','axon','myelin','synapse'];
let neuronSeq=[];
function missionBuild(){
  neuronSeq=[];
  const shuffled=[...NEURON_PARTS].sort(()=>Math.random()-0.5);
  document.getElementById('missionStage').innerHTML=`
   <div class="stage">
    <div class="stage-head">
      <div><div class="eyebrow" style="color:var(--violet)">Mission · Lesson 1</div><h2>Build a Neuron</h2>
      <p>A signal flows through a neuron in a fixed order. Tap the parts in the sequence the signal travels, from where it arrives to where it leaves.</p></div>
      <div class="mono" style="font-size:12px;color:var(--ink-faint)">+120 XP</div>
    </div>
    <svg viewBox="0 0 760 150" style="width:100%;height:auto;margin-bottom:22px;">
      <line id="nAxonBase" x1="60" y1="75" x2="700" y2="75" stroke="#EBE3EC" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round"/>
      <line id="nAxonLit" x1="60" y1="75" x2="700" y2="75" stroke="url(#ng)" stroke-width="10" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-dasharray="640" stroke-dashoffset="640"/>
      <circle id="nSpark" cx="60" cy="75" r="0" fill="#fff"/>
      <defs><linearGradient id="ng"><stop offset="0" stop-color="#7B6FD4"/><stop offset="1" stop-color="#DD8468"/></linearGradient></defs>
    </svg>
    <div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(5,1fr);gap:10px;margin-bottom:8px;" id="nSlots">
      ${NEURON_ORDER.map((_,i)=>`<div class="nslot" data-slot="${i}" style="aspect-ratio:1;border:2px dashed var(--line);border-radius:14px;display:grid;place-items:center;font-size:28px;color:var(--ink-faint);background:var(--paper);">${i+1}</div>`).join('')}
    </div>
    <p class="mono" style="font-size:11px;color:var(--ink-faint);text-align:center;margin-bottom:22px;">signal direction →</p>
    <div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:10px;justify-content:center;" id="nPool">
      ${shuffled.map(p=>`<button class="npart" data-id="${p.id}" style="display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;gap:4px;background:#fff;border:1.5px solid var(--line);border-radius:14px;padding:14px 18px;min-width:118px;transition:.15s;">
        <span style="font-size:26px;">${p.icon}</span><span style="font-weight:700;font-size:13.5px;">${p.label}</span>
        <span style="font-size:10.5px;color:var(--ink-faint);">${p.hint}</span></button>`).join('')}
    </div>
    <div class="reader-foot" style="justify-content:center;border:none;margin-top:24px;padding:0;">
      <button class="btn btn-primary" id="nFire" disabled style="opacity:.5">Fire the signal ⚡</button>
      <button class="btn btn-ghost" id="nReset">Reset</button>
    </div>
    <div id="nMsg" style="text-align:center;margin-top:16px;font-weight:700;"></div>
   </div>`;
  document.querySelectorAll('.npart').forEach(b=>b.addEventListener('click',()=>placeNeuronPart(b)));
  document.getElementById('nReset').addEventListener('click',missionBuild);
  document.getElementById('nFire').addEventListener('click',fireNeuron);
}
function placeNeuronPart(btn){
  if(btn.disabled)return;
  const id=btn.dataset.id, idx=neuronSeq.length;
  neuronSeq.push(id);
  const slot=document.querySelector(`.nslot[data-slot="${idx}"]`);
  const p=NEURON_PARTS.find(x=>x.id===id);
  slot.innerHTML=`<div style="font-size:26px">${p.icon}</div>`;
  slot.style.borderStyle='solid';slot.style.borderColor='var(--violet)';slot.style.background='var(--violet-soft)';
  btn.disabled=true;btn.style.opacity='.35';btn.style.pointerEvents='none';
  if(neuronSeq.length===5){const f=document.getElementById('nFire');f.disabled=false;f.style.opacity='1';}
}
function fireNeuron(){
  const correct=neuronSeq.every((id,i)=>id===NEURON_ORDER[i]);
  const msg=document.getElementById('nMsg');
  if(!correct){
    msg.style.color='var(--coral)';
    msg.textContent='Not quite, the signal flows Dendrites → Soma → Axon → Myelin → Synapse. Reset and try again.';
    document.getElementById('nSlots').animate([{transform:'translateX(0)'},{transform:'translateX(-8px)'},{transform:'translateX(8px)'},{transform:'translateX(0)'}],{duration:300});
    return;
  }
  msg.style.color='var(--green)';msg.textContent='Action potential propagating…';
  const lit=document.getElementById('nAxonLit'),spark=document.getElementById('nSpark');
  lit.animate([{strokeDashoffset:640},{strokeDashoffset:0}],{duration:1100,fill:'forwards'});
  spark.animate([{cx:60,r:9,opacity:1},{cx:700,r:9,opacity:1},{cx:700,r:22,opacity:0}],{duration:1200,fill:'forwards'});
  setTimeout(()=>{
    msg.textContent='Perfect neuron, signal delivered! 🎉';
    const isNew=completeMission('build','neuron_architect',120);
    if(!isNew)toast('🔬','Replayed','Neuron fired again','');
  },1250);
}

/* ── MISSION 3 · NEUROTRANSMITTER MYSTERY ── */
const CASES=[
  {file:'Case 01',vignette:'A 34-year-old reports losing all motivation. Activities that once felt rewarding now feel flat, and they struggle to start anything. Movement and memory are normal.',options:['Dopamine','GABA','Acetylcholine','Glutamate'],a:0,why:'Loss of reward and motivation points to the dopamine system, central to reward and drive.'},
  {file:'Case 02',vignette:'A patient experiences low mood, disrupted sleep, and changes in appetite over several months. A doctor prescribes an SSRI, which helps.',options:['Norepinephrine','Serotonin','Dopamine','GABA'],a:1,why:'Mood, sleep and appetite plus SSRI response implicate serotonin.'},
  {file:'Case 03',vignette:'Someone with severe anxiety is given a medication that calms the nervous system by enhancing the brain\'s main inhibitory signalling. They feel sedated.',options:['Glutamate','Dopamine','GABA','Acetylcholine'],a:2,why:'Enhancing inhibition and calming is the hallmark of GABA-boosting drugs.'},
  {file:'Case 04',vignette:'An older adult develops a resting tremor, stiffness, and slowed movement. Scans show loss of cells in a dopamine-producing midbrain region.',options:['Serotonin','Dopamine','GABA','Norepinephrine'],a:1,why:'Parkinsonian movement symptoms arise from loss of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra.'},
  {file:'Case 05',vignette:'A student pulling an all-nighter takes a stimulant and feels intensely alert and on edge, with a racing heart, the stress-and-arousal system in overdrive.',options:['GABA','Glutamate','Norepinephrine','Serotonin'],a:2,why:'Heightened alertness, arousal and stress response are driven by norepinephrine.'}
];
let caseIdx=0,caseScore=0;
function missionMystery(){caseIdx=0;caseScore=0;renderCase();}
function renderCase(){
  if(caseIdx>=CASES.length)return mysteryDone();
  const c=CASES[caseIdx];
  document.getElementById('missionStage').innerHTML=`
   <div class="stage">
    <div class="stage-head">
      <div><div class="eyebrow" style="color:var(--teal)">Mission · Lesson 3</div><h2>Neurotransmitter Mystery</h2>
      <p>Read each patient file and name the chemical messenger most likely involved.</p></div>
      <div class="mono" style="font-size:12px;color:var(--ink-faint)">${caseIdx+1} / ${CASES.length}</div>
    </div>
    <div class="callout case" style="margin-top:0;"><div class="ct">🗂️ ${c.file}</div><p style="font-size:15.5px;">${c.vignette}</p></div>
    <div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:11px;margin-top:18px;" id="caseOpts">
      ${c.options.map((o,i)=>`<button class="copt" data-i="${i}" style="background:#fff;border:1.5px solid var(--line);border-radius:13px;padding:16px;font-weight:700;font-size:15px;transition:.15s;">${o}</button>`).join('')}
    </div>
    <div id="caseWhy" style="display:none;border-radius:12px;padding:14px 16px;margin-top:16px;font-size:14px;"></div>
    <div class="reader-foot" style="border:none;padding:0;margin-top:18px;display:none;" id="caseNext">
      <button class="btn btn-primary" onclick="caseIdx++;renderCase();">${caseIdx===CASES.length-1?'See results →':'Next case →'}</button>
    </div>
   </div>`;
  document.querySelectorAll('.copt').forEach(b=>b.addEventListener('click',()=>{
    const i=+b.dataset.i,c2=CASES[caseIdx];
    document.querySelectorAll('.copt').forEach(x=>x.style.pointerEvents='none');
    document.querySelector(`.copt[data-i="${c2.a}"]`).style.cssText+='border-color:var(--green);background:var(--green-soft);';
    const why=document.getElementById('caseWhy');
    if(i===c2.a){caseScore++;why.style.background='var(--green-soft)';why.style.color='#3F7D5E';why.innerHTML='✓ '+c2.why;}
    else{b.style.cssText+='border-color:var(--coral);background:var(--coral-soft);';why.style.background='var(--coral-soft)';why.style.color='#B0614E';why.innerHTML='✗ '+c2.why;}
    why.style.display='block';document.getElementById('caseNext').style.display='flex';
  }));
}
function mysteryDone(){
  const xp=20*caseScore+(caseScore>=4?40:0);
  document.getElementById('missionStage').innerHTML=`
   <div class="stage" style="text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-size:60px;margin-bottom:10px;">🕵️</div>
    <h2 style="font-size:30px;">Case closed</h2>
    <p style="color:var(--ink-soft);margin:10px 0 22px;">You solved <b>${caseScore} of ${CASES.length}</b> cases correctly.</p>
    <div class="reader-foot" style="justify-content:center;border:none;padding:0;">
